Description and Findings\n\nPosterior Fossa:\n- Fourth Ventricle: Normal shape and size\n- Brainstem: Unremarkable\n- Cerebellum: Unremarkable\n\nMidline: Unremarkable\n\nSupratentorial Ventricular System: Normal shape and size\n\nCortical Sulci and Cisterns: Preserved\n\nCerebral Parenchyma: There is an intra-axial, cortico-subcortical, parieto-occipital lesion on the left side, appearing ovoid and slightly multilobulated. It shows an inhomogeneous signal, predominantly hyperintense on FLAIR and T1, with a probable subacute hemorrhagic component inside. On gradient echo sequences, it appears both hyperintense and hypointense, related to hemoglobin degradation products. There are signs of diffusion facilitation with a hyperintense component on the B1000 sequence inside. The described lesion shows irregular, predominantly peripheral enhancement. It measures approximately 32 mm anteroposteriorly, 31 mm in height, and 27 mm transversely. It is surrounded by hyperintense perilesional edema on FLAIR, causing effacement of adjacent cortical sulci and partial collapse of the occipital horn and atrium of the left lateral ventricle.\n\nImaging Comment:\nLeft cortico-subcortical parieto-occipital expansive lesion of probable secondary etiology. It presents with perilesional edema.

Description and Findings\n\nPosterior Fossa:\nFourth Ventricle: Normal shape and size\nBrainstem: Flair sequence shows hyperintensity in the pontine white matter, suggestive of chronic microvascular changes.\nCerebellum: Unremarkable\nMidline: Unremarkable\nSupratentorial Ventricular System: Moderately enlarged. Evans index 0.50.\nDynamic CSF flow sequence confirms passage through the aqueduct of Sylvius.\nMeasurement values are attached (Mean flux x heart rate: 4.27).\nCortical Sulci and Cisterns: Widened at the level of the Sylvian cisterns and perisylvian cortical sulci.\nCerebral Parenchyma: Flair sequence shows hyperintensity in the periventricular and deep white matter, likely of chronic vascular origin (Fazekas 2).\nNo evidence of recent vascular lesions on diffusion sequence.\n\nImaging Comment:\nVentricular dilation with the previously described characteristics, without signs of progression, suggesting exclusion of normal pressure hydrocephalus (NPH).\nMild white matter changes likely of chronic vascular origin.\nNo evidence of recent vascular lesions.
Posterior fossa:\n-Fourth ventricle: Normal shape and size\n-Brainstem: Unremarkable\n-Cerebellum: Hyperintense lesion on Flair and T2 sequences and hypointense on T1 in the basal part of the right cerebellar hemisphere. It is markedly hyperintense on B 1000, with signal drop on ADC mapping, consistent with an acute ischemic lesion. In its anterior part, there is a hypointense area on gradient echo sequence related to the presence of blood degradation products.\nMidline: Unremarkable\nSupratentorial ventricular system: Normal shape and size\nCortical sulci and cisterns: Preserved\nCerebral parenchyma: Presence of multiple punctate hyperintense images in the deep white matter of both hemispheres with a chronic vascular appearance (Fazekas 2).\n\nAngiographic sequence:\nIntracranial internal carotid arteries: Unremarkable\nAnterior cerebral arteries: Unremarkable, hypoplasia of the A1 segment of the left anterior cerebral artery (anatomical variant).\nMiddle cerebral arteries: Unremarkable\nVertebrobasilar system: No flow signal identified in the proximal V4 segment of the right vertebral artery. The distal segment of V4 shows flow signal with apparent retrograde filling.\nThe left vertebral artery shows preserved flow. Partial visualization of the right PICA. Preserved flow in the left PICA.\nLeft posterior communicating artery is patent.\n\nImaging comment:\nAcute ischemic lesion in the right cerebellar hemisphere, with the presence of blood degradation products in its anterior part.\nAbsence of flow signal in the proximal V4 segment of the right vertebral artery.\nChronic vascular-appearing white matter changes.
Description and Findings:\nThere is spontaneous hyperdensity corresponding to blood content in the cerebellopontine, prebulbar, prepontine cisterns, and basal cisterns, as well as in both Sylvian fissures, cortical sulci of the convexity, and interhemispheric fissure. Signs of intraventricular extension and early dilation of the supratentorial ventricular system are observed. A small spontaneously hyperdense lesion with adjacent hypodensity in the right lateral frontal region is compatible with a hemorrhagic contusion. The rest of the parenchyma shows no densitometric alterations.\n\nImaging Comment:\nSigns of subarachnoid hemorrhage with intraventricular extension (Fisher grade 4).\n\nDescription and Findings:\nRight internal carotid artery: Unremarkable.\nRight anterior cerebral artery: Unremarkable.\nRight middle cerebral artery: Unremarkable.\n\nLeft internal carotid artery: Unremarkable.\nLeft anterior cerebral artery: Unremarkable.\nLeft middle cerebral artery: Unremarkable.\n\nRight vertebral artery: Unremarkable.\nLeft vertebral artery: Dominant. At the topography of the origin of the left PICA, there is an irregular saccular dilation approximately 8 mm in maximum diameter. The PICA arises from this dilation.\nBasilar trunk: Unremarkable.\nRight posterior cerebral artery: Unremarkable.\nLeft posterior cerebral artery: Unremarkable.\n\nImaging Comment:\nIrregular saccular aneurysmal dilation at the origin of the left PICA in the vertebral artery, as previously described. Preserved flow in the remaining vascular structures of the circle of Willis.

Description and Findings\n\nPosterior Fossa:\n- Fourth Ventricle: Normal shape and size\n- Brainstem: Unremarkable\n- Cerebellum: Unremarkable\n\nMidline: Intact\n\nCerebral Parenchyma: Multiple bilateral cortico-subcortical lesions are observed in the frontal, parietal, and temporal regions, with mixed density, predominantly hypodense, and areas of spontaneous hyperdensity related to hemorrhagic and edematous contusions. These cause generalized effacement of cortical sulci and partial collapse of the lateral ventricles. \n\nSigns of ventricular shift are noted at the level of the body of the left lateral ventricle and both occipital horns.\n\nThere is spontaneous hyperdensity at the level of the falx cerebri related to a subdural hematoma measuring 7 mm in thickness.\n\nOn bone window, multiple fracture lines are observed bilaterally in the frontal region, involving the floor of both frontal sinuses and the roof of both orbits, with segments displaced caudally on the right side, causing compression of the eyeball.\n\nA fracture line is noted in the left superior frontal region extending to involve the lateral wall of the orbit and ipsilateral anterior mastoid air cells, without involvement of the middle or inner ear.\n\nBilateral parietal fracture lines are present.\n\nThere is evidence of an entry and exit bullet trajectory in the right frontal and left fronto-parietal regions, with displaced bone fragments at this level. Underlying these, there are laminar subdural collections measuring 5 mm on the left and 4 mm on the right.\n\nOccupation of the left middle ear and mastoid air cells is observed.\n\nSigns of hemosinus are present in the frontal sinuses and ethmoid air cells.

Description and Findings\n\nPosterior Fossa:\n- Cerebellum: There is an intraparenchymal space-occupying lesion in the right cerebellar hemisphere. The lesion is heterogeneous, predominantly hypointense on FLAIR and T1, with intense enhancement on post-gadolinium series. It presents some small pseudo-cystic areas that are hyperintense on T2 and FLAIR and hypointense on T1 without enhancement. It measures approximately 38 x 25 mm in its largest axial diameter, 36 mm in the anteroposterior direction, and 27 mm in height. The lesion is surrounded by perilesional vasogenic edema that is hyperintense on T2 and FLAIR. The lesion exerts a mild mass effect on the fourth ventricle and causes slight caudal displacement of the right cerebellar tonsil into the foramen magnum. Two small nodular enhancement foci are observed at the right posterobasal level measuring 5 mm and at the right anterosuperior cerebellar level measuring 4 mm. The rest of the cerebellum shows no significant abnormalities.\n- Brainstem: Unremarkable\n- Midline: Unremarkable\n- Supratentorial Ventricular System: Normal shape and size\n- Cortical Sulci and Cisterns: Preserved\n- Cerebral Parenchyma: Normal morphology and signal intensity in both gray and white matter\nIn the post-gadolinium series, no other pathological parenchymal or meningeal enhancements are observed.\n\nImaging Comment:\nRight cerebellar intraparenchymal lesion with a heterogeneous appearance, showing intense post-contrast enhancement and surrounded by perilesional vasogenic edema. It exerts a mild mass effect on the fourth ventricle and causes caudal displacement of the right cerebellar tonsil. Small satellite enhancing nodules in the right cerebellar hemisphere.

Description and Findings  \nA large expansive lesion is observed in the left paramedian occipitoparietal region, involving part of the splenium of the corpus callosum. This lesion appears hyperintense on T2 and FLAIR sequences and hypointense on T1, with hypointensity on gradient echo sequences related to the presence of blood degradation products.  \nIt shows intense and heterogeneous enhancement, predominantly peripheral, on post-contrast series, with a central necrotic area.  \nThe lesion measures approximately 82 mm anteroposteriorly, 43 mm transversely, and 66 mm in height.  \nOn diffusion sequence, there is peripheral hyperintensity on B 1000 with decreased signal on ADC mapping, indicating increased cellularity.  \nPerfusion sequence was performed, showing increased relative cerebral blood volume at the periphery, particularly in its medial portion.  \nThere is a mass effect evidenced by the collapse of the occipital horn of the left lateral ventricle and the left ventricular atrium, as well as the occipitoparietal cortical sulci on the left side.  \nThere is a 5 mm shift of the midline to the right.  \nThe rest of the ventricular system is slightly enlarged, particularly the temporal extensions of the lateral ventricles, predominantly on the left.  \nThere is accentuation of the amplitude of the remaining cortical sulci and cisterns. Punctate hyperintense images in the periventricular and deep white matter suggest a probable chronic vascular etiology.  \nPosterior Fossa:  \n- Fourth ventricle: Normal shape and size  \n- Brainstem: Unremarkable  \n- Cerebellum: Unremarkable  \n\nAngiographic Sequence:  \nIntracranial internal carotid arteries: Unremarkable  \nAnterior cerebral arteries: Unremarkable  \nMiddle cerebral arteries: Unremarkable  \nVertebrobasilar system: Unremarkable  \n\nImaging Comment:  \nExpansive lesion in the left occipitoparietal region with the previously described characteristics, suggesting a primary lesion of probable high-grade glial etiology. Preserved flow in the vascular structures of the circle of Willis.

Indication: Malunion of the right femoral neck.  \nImaging Technician:  \nSecretary:  \n\nMRI OF THE PELVIS  \n\nTechnique:  \nAxial T1, coronal T1, axial T2, coronal T2, and coronal STIR sequences were performed without contrast injection.  \n\nResults  \n\nThere is evidence of shortening of the left femoral neck with a heterogeneous appearance, showing hyperintensity on T1, T2, and STIR sequences.  \nThere is posteromedial slippage of the left femoral head, appearing hyperintense on STIR, and thinned, measuring 15mm compared to 26mm on the right side.  \nThis is associated with a reduction of the left cervico-femoral angle, measured at 91° compared to 133° on the right.  \nThe sphericity of the left femoral head is preserved.  \nAn osteophyte is noted on the left greater trochanter.  \nNo intra-articular effusion is present.  \nOn the right, the sphericity of the femoral head is preserved.  \nNo acetabular abnormalities.  \nNo joint effusion.  \nNo abnormalities of the sacroiliac and symphyseal joints.  \n\nCONCLUSION  \n\nMRI findings suggest shortening of the left femoral neck with heterogeneous signal and posteromedial epiphyseal slippage, along with a reduction of the cervico-femoral angle: these features suggest possible post-traumatic sequelae of the left femoral neck or epiphyseal remodeling.  \nNo abnormalities in the hip joints.
Indication: Suspected sacral bone metastasis from prostate cancer on CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis.\n\nPelvic MRI\n\nTechnique: \nT1 and T2 sequences were performed in three planes with gadolinium-enhanced T1 in three planes.\n\nResults:\n\nA signal anomaly is noted in the sacral segments, appearing as hyperintense on T1 and T2, with slight enhancement after gadolinium injection. \nNo signal anomaly is observed in the bilateral iliac wings.\nSignal anomalies are present in the vertebral bodies of L3 and L5, as well as the right femoral head.\nNo signal anomaly is seen in other vertebral structures or the contralateral femoral head.\nThe spherical shape of the femoral head is preserved.\nThe articular surfaces of the pelvis are intact.\nNo abnormalities in the periarticular soft tissues.\n\nConclusion:\n\nMRI findings suggest a signal anomaly in the sacral segments, vertebral bodies L3 and L5, and the right femoral head, consistent with bone metastases. \nNo abnormalities in the soft tissues or other lumbar spondylodiscal and osteoarticular structures of the pelvis.

Indication: Right-sided hemiparesis with brachio-facial predominance  \nImaging Technician:  \nSecretary:  \n\nBRAIN MRI  \n\nTechnique:  \nDiffusion sequences, T1, T2, and FLAIR sequences, and a 3D TOF sequence in the axial plane were performed with gadolinium injection.  \n\nResult  \n\n** Supratentorial Level  \n\nThere is a FLAIR, T2, diffusion hyperintense area in the left fronto-parietal cortico-subcortical region involving the lenticular nucleus, caudate nucleus, and ipsilateral internal capsule, exerting a slight mass effect on the ipsilateral lateral ventricle.  \nThis lesion area is isointense on T1 and T2* and slightly displaces the falx structure to the right by 3mm.  \nDilation of the ventricular system, particularly the lateral ventricles, with a rounded appearance of the occipital horns and dilation of the third ventricle measuring 9mm.  \nDiffuse widening of cortical sulci.  \nNo pathological pericerebral fluid collection.  \n\n** Infratentorial Level  \n\nNo morphological abnormalities of the bulbopontine region and cerebellar hemispheres.  \nThe fourth ventricle has a normal high morphology.  \nNo abnormality of the cisterna magna.  \nThe prepontine cisterns are without abnormality.  \nSinus cavities of the face and skull base are without abnormalities.  \n\nCONCLUSION  \n\nMRI appearance of an acute ischemic stroke in the superficial and deep territory of the left middle and anterior cerebral arteries, with mass effect on the left lateral ventricle without signs of brain herniation.  \nChronic adult hydrocephalus associated with diffuse cortical atrophy.  \nThe posterior fossa is normal.  \nSinus cavities of the face and skull base are without abnormalities.
Indication: Evaluation for cervical spinal cord compression syndrome.\n\nCERVICAL SPINE MRI\n\nTechnique:\nSagittal T1, T2, and axial 3D BASG sequences were performed without gadolinium contrast.\n\nFindings:\nOn sagittal sequences:\n- Mild reversal of cervical lordosis is observed.\n- Grade I pancake-like compression of C5 and C6 is noted.\n- Presence of anterior vertebral body osteophytes from C4 to C6.\n- Presence of posterior vertebral body osteophytes at C5 and C6.\n- Posterior disc protrusion at C5-C6 impacting the adjacent spinal cord.\n- Disc degeneration is indicated by hypointense appearance on T2-weighted images.\n- No signal abnormality of the spinal cord.\n\nOn axial sequences:\n\n**At C2-C3 and C3-C4:\n- No global or focal disc protrusion suggestive of a disc herniation.\n- No abnormalities of the ligamenta flava. No foraminal stenosis.\n- Normal anteroposterior canal dimensions.\n\n**At C4-C5:\n- No global or focal disc protrusion.\n- No abnormalities of the ligamenta flava. No foraminal stenosis.\n- Normal anteroposterior canal dimensions.\n\n**At C5-C6:\n- Posterior median hard disc herniation in conflict with the adjacent spinal cord and C6 ventral roots.\n- No abnormalities of the ligamenta flava. No foraminal stenosis.\n\n**At C6-C7:\n- No global or focal disc protrusion.\n- No abnormalities of the ligamenta flava. No foraminal stenosis.\n\nCONCLUSION:\nMRI evidence of a subligamentous median hard disc herniation at C5-C6 in conflict with the adjacent spinal cord and C6 ventral roots, without any identified spinal cord distress at this time.\nMild reversal of cervical lordosis.\nDegenerative spondylotic changes from C3 to C6.
Indication: Bilateral C5-C6 cervicobrachial neuralgia  \nImaging Technicians:  \nSecretary:  \n\nMRI OF THE CERVICAL SPINE  \n\nTechnique:  \nSagittal T1, T2, and axial 3D BASG sequences were performed without gadolinium injection.  \n\nResults:  \nOn sagittal sequences:  \nThere is a straightening of the cervical spine.  \nNo signal or height abnormalities of the vertebral bodies.  \nDisc degeneration is noted by a hypointense appearance of the discs on T2.  \nThere is a posterior subligamentous disc protrusion observed at C3-C4 and C6-C7.  \nA slight signal anomaly of the spinal cord at C3-C4, suggestive of edema.  \n\nOn axial sequences:  \n\n**At C2-C3:**  \nNo global or focal disc protrusion suggestive of a disc herniation.  \nNo abnormalities of the ligamenta flava. No foraminal abnormalities.  \n\n**At C6-C7:**  \nMedian disc herniation partially contacting the spinal cord at this level.  \nNo global or focal disc protrusion.  \nNo abnormalities of the ligamenta flava. No foraminal abnormalities.  \n\nElsewhere, no abnormalities are noted on the myelographic sequences.  \n\nCONCLUSION:  \nMRI evidence of subligamentous median disc herniation observed at C3-C4, C4-C5, and C6-C7, with conflict with the spinal cord at C3-C4 and C5-C6, with early signs of distress at C3-C4.  \nNo foraminal abnormalities.

Indication: Evaluation of a chronic swelling in the left ankle.  \nImaging Technician:  \nSecretary:  \n\nMRI / ULTRASOUND OF THE LEFT ANKLE  \n\nTechnique:  \nA proton density sequence was performed in three planes, followed by Dixon sequences and a STIR sequence in the sagittal plane centered on the ankle. This examination was correlated with ultrasound findings.  \n\nFindings  \n\nTendinous and Ligamentous Structures  \n\nThere is a very slight thickening with minimal structural changes of the flexor digitorum longus tendon in the medial and tibial retro-malleolar region. This is accompanied by a minimal peritendinous fluid collection.  \nNo abnormalities are noted in the flexor hallucis longus tendon.  \nNo abnormalities in the peroneus brevis and longus tendons.  \nNo abnormalities observed in the various retinacula.  \nRegarding the Achilles tendon, there is a slight alteration at its insertion on the calcaneus with a hyperintense signal focus.  \nUltrasound reveals small foci of calcification.  \n\nOsseous and Articular Structures  \n\nThere is a minimal intra-articular tibio-talar fluid collection without any observed lesions in the surrounding structures, including the distal tibia and fibula, and the talo-calcaneal structure.  \n\nVenous Structures  \n\nNo evidence of venous thrombosis affecting the superficial or deep regions.  \n\nCONCLUSION  \n\nMRI / Ultrasound findings suggest chronic tendinitis involving the flexor digitorum longus tendons in the medial and tibial retro-malleolar region of the left ankle, with a minimal peritendinous fluid collection.  \nMinimal intra-articular tibio-talar fluid collection.  \nVery mild calcific tendinitis of the Achilles tendon at its insertion on the calcaneus.  \nNo abnormalities in the venous structures of the ankle.

Indication: Bone tumor with soft tissue involvement.  \nImaging Technicians:  \nSecretary:  \n\nMRI OF THE LEFT SHOULDER  \n\nTechnique:  \nProton density T1 sequences with Fat Sat in coronal, sagittal, and axial planes, followed by coronal T1 with gadolinium contrast injection.\n\nFindings  \nOsteoarticular findings:  \nA lytic lesion is identified in the proximal metaphyseal-epiphyseal region of the left humerus, measuring 23.9mm x 20mm x 21mm. The lesion matrix shows T2 hyperintensity, T1 hypointensity, and does not enhance post-contrast.  \nThere is cortical breach on the anterior aspect of the lesion with a small subdeltoid fluid collection.  \nThe humeral head maintains its spherical shape.  \nThere is periosteal reaction adjacent to the lesion.  \nNo abnormalities are noted in other bony structures, including the clavicle and scapula.  \n\nTendinous-muscular findings:  \nA subdeltoid fluid collection is present without any signal abnormality in the muscle.  \nNo abnormalities are noted in other muscular structures, including the rotator cuff.  \nNo signal abnormalities in other muscular structures of the left shoulder.  \n\nCONCLUSION  \n\nMRI appearance of a rapidly evolving lytic lesion in the proximal metaphyseal-epiphyseal region of the left humerus, causing cortical breach with a small subdeltoid fluid collection.  \nNo involvement of other musculo-tendinous structures of the shoulder.  \nNo abnormalities in the scapula or clavicle.
Indication: ACL Tear?\n\nLEFT KNEE MRI\n\nTechnique:\nSequences were performed in three planes using proton density Fat Sat, followed by axial T1, sagittal T2, and coronal Dixon sequences.\n\nFindings\n\n**Menisco-ligamentous assessment\nThe analysis of the various sequences reveals a horizontal fissure lesion extending to the joint, classified as a type III Stoller and Crues lesion of the posterior horn of the medial meniscus.\nPartial tear of the anterior cruciate ligament.\nNo fissure observed in other meniscal structures.\nNo abnormalities of the posterior cruciate ligament.\nThe quadriceps tendon and patellar ligaments are also without abnormalities.\nNo abnormalities of the collateral ligaments.\n\n**Osteo-articular assessment\nSatisfactory bone mineralization.\nNo signal abnormalities in the various bony structures of the knee, except for a few sclerotic foci affecting the distal end of the femur.\nNo narrowing of the femorotibial and femoropatellar joint spaces.\nNo osteophytes on the femoral condyles, tibial plateaus, or patellar edges.\nNo hypertrophy of the tibial spines.\nSubluxation of the femoropatellar joint space by approximately 1 cm.\nMinimal periarticular fluid effusion.\nAnterior sub-meniscal cyst measuring approximately 6 mm in its longest axis.\n\nCONCLUSION\n\nMRI evidence of a partial tear of the anterior cruciate ligament associated with a type III Stoller and Crues horizontal fissure of the posterior horn of the left medial meniscus.\nSubluxation of the femoropatellar joint space by approximately 1 cm associated with minimal intra-articular fluid effusion.\nAnterior sub-meniscal cyst measuring approximately 6 mm in its longest axis.\nIntact posterior cruciate ligament.\nNo abnormalities of the quadriceps tendon, patellar tendon, and collateral ligaments.

No abnormalities are observed in the femorotibial and femoropatellar joint spaces. The articular cartilage appears normal.\n\n**Meniscal and Ligamentous Structures\n\nThere is evidence of remodeling of the lateral collateral ligament, with a signal anomaly at its juxta-tibial segment, appearing as a mild hyperintensity on Fat Sat. A type II Stoller and Crue fissure lesion is present in the posterior horn of the lateral meniscus. No abnormalities are noted in the central pivot ligaments or the medial collateral ligament. The quadriceps tendons and patellar ligaments are also unremarkable.\n\nCONCLUSION\n\nMRI findings suggest a strain of the lateral collateral ligament of the left knee. Indication: Contusion and partial tear of the right medial collateral ligament on knee ultrasound + fluid effusion\n\nLEFT KNEE MRI\n\nTechnique:\nSequences were performed in three planes using proton density Fat Sat, followed by axial T1, sagittal T2, and coronal Dixon sequences.\n\nResults\n**Menisco-ligamentous findings\nThere is a significant horizontal tear, classified as type III, extending to the joint surface of the posterior horn of the lateral meniscus, and a type II horizontal tear of the anterior horn of the lateral meniscus.\nNo abnormalities are noted in the central pivot ligaments or the collateral ligaments.\nThe patellar tendon and patellar ligament appear normal.\n**Osteo-articular findings\nThere is a unicortical fracture at the inferior margin of the patella.\nNo abnormalities are noted at the margins of the distal femur, proximal fibula, or tibia.\nThere is a small amount of intra-articular fluid collection.\n\nCONCLUSION\n\nMRI findings indicate a type III Stoller and Crues tear of the posterior horn of the left lateral meniscus and a type II tear of the anterior horn of the left lateral meniscus, with a moderate intra-articular fluid collection associated with minimal reactive hemarthrosis.\nUnicortical fracture of the inferior margin of the patella.\nNo abnormalities in the ligamentous structures, including the anterior and posterior cruciate ligaments, collateral ligaments, and patellar ligaments.
Indication: Large mass straddling segments II and III  \nImaging Technician:  \nSecretary:  \n\nLIVER MRI\n\nTechnique  \nAxial and coronal T1, T2 sequences were performed, followed by a dynamic sequence and a T1 gadolinium sequence.\n\nResult  \nAnalysis of the various sequences reveals a liver of normal size, measuring 14 cm in FLESH, with slightly irregular contours and a heterogeneous structure altered by the presence of a well-defined tissue formation. This formation appears as heterogeneous T1 hypointense, heterogeneous T2 hyperintense with peripheral enhancement 5 minutes after contrast injection, increasing after 10 minutes and becoming homogeneous after 15 minutes. This formation is located in segments II and III.  \nThe portal vein is patent and of normal caliber.  \nNo abnormalities in the intrahepatic bile ducts.  \nThe gallbladder is normal.  \nThe pancreas is of normal size with no signal abnormalities observed.  \nThe spleen is of normal size and morphostructure.  \nThe kidneys are of normal size, maintaining their physiological functions without lithiasis formation or abnormal intraparenchymal contrast enhancement.  \nNo ascites or deep lymphadenopathy.\n\nCONCLUSION\n\nMRI appearance of a well-defined tissue formation with clear margins, presenting as heterogeneous T1 hypointense, heterogeneous T2 hyperintense, and showing ring enhancement 5 minutes after gadolinium injection, increasing after 10 minutes and becoming homogeneous after 15 minutes: findings suggestive of a hepatic hemangioma in segments II and III.  \nBiliary, pancreatic, splenic, and renal morphostructure without abnormalities.  \nNo ascites or deep lymphadenopathy.

Indication: Lumbar spinal stenosis.\n\nLUMBAR SPINE MRI\n\nTechnique:\n\nSagittal T1, T2, and axial 3D BASG sequences were performed without gadolinium contrast.\n\nFindings:\n\nOn sagittal and coronal sequences:\n- There is a straightening of the lumbar spine.\n- Notable disc degeneration is observed with a hypointense signal of the L4-L5 disc on T2.\n- Disc collapse is noted at L4-L5.\n- Subchondral cysts are present in the vertebral endplates of L4-L5.\n- No signal or height abnormalities of the vertebral bodies.\n- No signal abnormalities of the posterior wall.\n\nOn axial sequences:\n\n**At L2-L3:**\n- No global or focal disc protrusion suggestive of a herniated disc.\n- No abnormalities of the ligamenta flava.\n- No canal dimension abnormalities.\n- Facet joint osteoarthritis.\n\n**At L3-L4:**\n- A global disc bulge is noted, effacing the anterior epidural fat and contacting the dural sac.\n- No abnormalities of the ligamenta flava.\n- No canal dimension abnormalities.\n- Facet joint osteoarthritis.\n\n**At L4-L5:**\n- A global disc bulge is in conflict with the bilateral supraforaminal nerve root emergence of L5.\n- Thickening of the ligamenta flava.\n- No canal dimension abnormalities.\n- Facet joint osteoarthritis.\n\n**At L5-S1:**\n- Minimal global disc bulge not in conflict with nerve root emergence.\n- No abnormalities of the ligamenta flava.\n- No canal dimension abnormalities.\n- Facet joint osteoarthritis.\n\nCONCLUSION:\n\nMRI evidence of a narrowed lumbar canal secondary to diffuse minimal disc protrusions at L3-L4 and L5-S1, more pronounced at L4-L5, conflicting with the supraforaminal nerve root emergence of L5 and the adjacent dural sac. This condition is associated with thickening of the ligamenta flava at L4-L5, contributing to the increased canal narrowing at this level. Facet joint osteoarthritis is present from L2-L3 to L5-S1.

Diffuse disc protrusion from L3-L4 to L5-S1, contacting the dural sac at L3-L4 and L4-L5. Staged zygapophyseal joint osteoarthritis from L3-L4 to L5-S1.
Indication: Patient underwent surgery in 2019 for cauda equina syndrome due to a narrow lumbar canal with a large L3-L4 disc herniation.\n\nLUMBAR SPINE MRI\n\nTechnique:\n\nSagittal T1, T2, axial T2 STIR, and FAT SAT sequences were performed without gadolinium contrast.\n\nFindings\n\nOn the sagittal sequence:\n\nThere is a relatively straight lumbar spine alignment.\nThere is multilevel disc collapse, more pronounced at L3-L4 and L4-L5.\nDisc dehydration is indicated by hypointense T2 signal, particularly at L3-L4 and L4-L5.\nThere is spondylodesis from L3 to L5 with osteosynthesis hardware in place.\n\nOn the axial sequences:\n\n**At L2-L3,**\nThere is no global or focal disc protrusion. No zygapophyseal joint osteoarthritis.\nNo abnormalities of the ligamenta flava.\nThe anteroposterior canal diameter is 14mm at L2.\n\n**At L3-L4,**\nThere is a global disc bulge without distinct disc-root conflict.\nNo abnormalities of the ligamenta flava. No signs of fibrosis.\n\n**At L4-L5,**\nThere is a minimal disc bulge contacting the dural sac without distinct disc-root conflict. The appearance of the lateral paravertebral soft tissues is likely altered.\nNo signs of fibrosis.\n\n**At L5-S1,**\nThere is a minimal global disc bulge with a left paramedian herniation abutting the ipsilateral S1 root.\nNo abnormalities of the ligamenta flava. No canal diameter abnormalities.\nNo zygapophyseal joint osteoarthritis.\n\nCONCLUSION\n\nMRI evidence of spondylodesis from L2 to L5 with osteosynthesis hardware in place.\nMinimal diffuse disc protrusion from L3-L4 to L5-S1 partially contacting the dural sac at L4-L5 without distinct disc-root conflict.\nLeft paramedian herniation at L5-S1 with relative ipsilateral root conflict.\nNo signs of fibrosis noted.

Diagnostic query: "Incidental ultrasound finding of a focal liver lesion."\n\nTechnical data: Examination performed with a high-field (3T) MRI scanner, using T2 TSE, SPIR, DUAL FFE, DWI, mDIXONquant sequences, and mDIXON before and after intravenous administration of 10 ml of extracellular contrast agent.\n\nReport\nMRI UPPER ABDOMEN\nThe liver is of normal size and shape, with an altered signal intensity focus in the VII segment, where there is an oval formation with lobulated margins, measuring up to 4 x 3.8 cm. It appears hypointense on T1-weighted images and markedly hyperintense on T2-weighted images. After contrast administration, the lesion shows peripheral globular enhancement with a centripetal pattern, almost completely filling in the late phase acquired at 10 minutes, consistent with a cavernous hemangioma.\n\nAdditionally, in the VI segment, in a subcapsular location, there are three other oval formations with maximum diameters of 1.6 cm, 0.8 cm, and 0.5 cm, respectively. The largest has similar intensity characteristics; the two smaller ones show homogeneous enhancement in the arterial phase and are isointense with the vessels in subsequent phases, consistent with capillary hemangiomas.\n\nAdjacent areas show some hypervascular regions visible only in the arterial phase, compatible with areas of altered perfusion.\n\nThe gallbladder is normal in shape, without endoluminal signal intensity defects suggestive of lithiasis. The intrahepatic and extrahepatic bile ducts are not dilated.\n\nThe portal vein and its tributaries are patent and of regular caliber.\n\nNo significant abnormalities in the spleen, pancreas, adrenal glands, and kidneys (a small cortical cyst on the right).\n\nNo significantly enlarged lymph nodes.\n\nConclusions: Presence of two focal liver lesions compatible with cavernous hemangiomas (VII-VI segment) and two focal lesions compatible with capillary hemangiomas (VI segment).

Clinical question: evaluation in a patient with adrenal adenoma\n\nThe intrahepatic and extrahepatic bile ducts are not dilated, and there are no signal voids indicative of stones. The common bile duct has a maximum diameter of 8 mm. The gallbladder is overdistended and free of stones. The cystic duct follows a retrocholedochal course, emptying at the junction between the proximal and middle segments of the common bile duct. The pancreatic duct (Wirsung's duct) is not dilated. The spleen, pancreas, right adrenal gland, liver, and kidneys appear normal on the acquired MRI sequences. The left adrenal gland shows a 25 mm mass characterized by signal drop in the dual "out-of-phase" sequences due to intracellular lipid content, consistent with an adenoma. No fluid collections or lymphadenopathy are observed at the examined levels.
Clinical question: follow-up in a patient with known FNH\n\nIn the liver, within segment 4, there is a large nodular formation with lobulated margins, measuring 76 x 62 mm. It shows a faint hyperintensity on T2-weighted images, with no diffusion signal increase. On dynamic study, it exhibits some hypervascular spots in the arterial phase, while appearing isointense in the portal and delayed phases. During the hepatobiliary excretion phase, it is characterized by a peripheral hyperintense area and a central hypointense component, consistent with a scar typical of FNH.  \nThe gallbladder is normally distended and contains a minimal amount of biliary sludge; the bile ducts are not dilated.  \nThe pancreas, spleen, adrenal glands, and kidneys show normal MRI signal characteristics.  \nNo abdominal fluid collections or enlarged lymph nodes are observed at the examined levels.

At the border of the lower quadrants of the left breast, there are two lesions that persistently accumulate contrast, measuring up to 1.2 cm in the middle and posterior thirds, likely part of a fibroadenomatous change, while the more anterior lesion could correspond to a lymph node. In the lower outer quadrant of the right breast, there is a postoperative scar with an adjacent lamellar T2 hyperintense area consistent with a seroma. No other morphologically suspicious lesions, pathological contrast enhancement, or areas of diffusion restriction are found. BIRADS 3 - normal postoperative findings on the right. A second-look ultrasound is recommended for the changes in the left breast.

An MRI was performed using standard imaging techniques with a multiparametric protocol, including T2, DWI, and DCE sequences. The breasts are very dense, of a glandular-mixed type, ACR type C. There is mild background enhancement of the contrast agent. Bilaterally, in all quadrants, oval lesions with smooth contours are visible, T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense, which predominantly show persistent contrast uptake post-contrast, with some lesions exhibiting a plateau type dynamic curve. Most of the described lesions show dark internal septa that do not imbibe post-contrast and morphologically correspond primarily to fibroadenomas. The lesions measure up to 2 cm in diameter. Compared to the previous MRI, there are no significant changes. No stellate lesions or non-mass enhancement are visible. BIRADS 3. Multiple fibroadenomas bilaterally. Strong fibrocystic changes. A second-look ultrasound is recommended, and depending on the findings, the need for a core biopsy should be determined.

MRI OF THE BRAIN AND ORBITS\n\nTECHNIQUE: Transverse, coronal, and sagittal images were obtained using T1-weighted sequences before and after fat signal suppression, transverse T2-weighted sequences, GRE, heavily T2-weighted sequences with CSF signal suppression (FLAIR), as well as transverse images using Diffusion Weighted Imaging. Additionally, transverse and coronal images of the orbits were acquired using T1 and T2-weighted sequences with fat signal suppression, along with transverse proton density images.\n\nFINDINGS:\nThere is a solid mass with lobulated margins in the vitreous body of the right eye, with a broad base at the posterior inferior part of the sclera and possible infiltration of it, with thickening of the adjacent sclera, although without evident pathological enhancement. No obvious extraocular extension is observed. The lesion shows intermediate signal intensity on T1 and T2-weighted sequences and measures 9mm x 7mm x 13.4mm (transverse x craniocaudal x oblique anteroposterior). The lesion exhibits mild contrast enhancement and significant restriction of molecular diffusion, indicative of increased cellularity. These findings are suggestive of a neoplastic process, likely retinoblastoma. The lesion is anatomically close to the optic disc, developing anteriorly and inferiorly to it, without evidence of optic nerve extension in this examination. Clinical correlation is advised.\n\nNormal appearance of the left optic nerve and left eye, as well as the extraocular muscles and retrobulbar fat bilaterally.\n\nNo significant areas of abnormal signal intensity or pathological enhancement are identified in the cerebral hemispheres, brainstem, or cerebellum.\n\nThe ventricular system, peripheral subarachnoid space, and cisterns are within normal limits for age in terms of size and morphology.\n\nNo significant findings in the paranasal sinuses and the anatomical structures of the skull base.
BRAIN MRI\n\nTECHNIQUE: Axial, coronal, and sagittal slices were obtained in T1-weighted orientation, along with axial slices in T2-weighted, GRE, heavily T2-weighted with CSF signal suppression (FLAIR), and axial slices using Diffusion Weighted Imaging.\n\nFINDINGS:\n\nNo areas of abnormal signal are observed in the cerebral hemispheres, brainstem, or cerebellum. There is a degree of symmetrical widening of the peripheral subarachnoid space, suggestive of mild central brain atrophy. The ventricular system and cisterns of the brain appear within normal limits for age, without signs of compression, displacement, or pathological dilation. A small amount of fluid is noted within some of the posterior mastoid air cells. A retention cyst is present in the right sphenoid sinus, and there are thickened mucosal changes in both maxillary sinuses, indicative of chronic sinusitis. No other pathological findings are noted in the anatomical structures of the skull base. No apparent pathology is observed in the prepontine segment of the cranial nerves (III-VIII).

MRI OF THE RIGHT KNEE\n\nTECHNIQUE: The examination was performed using a 3 Tesla MRI system. T1 and PD/fs sequences were obtained in three planes.\n\nFINDINGS:\nThe body of the medial meniscus appears subluxed, with increased internal signal intensity and significant swelling, indicative of severe degeneration/degenerative tear. No degenerative changes or tears are observed in the lateral meniscus.\n\nNo pathological findings were detected in the cruciate ligaments or the lateral and medial collateral ligaments.\n\nThere is a noted area of articular cartilage loss measuring 0.4 cm with an underlying small focus of bone marrow edema around the midsection of the medial tibial condyle, suggestive of degenerative origin (ICRS grade 4). Partial-thickness cartilage loss is also present in the lateral compartment. These findings are indicative of osteoarthritis.\n\nA moderate amount of joint effusion is observed.\n\nThere is a high origin of the anterior tibial artery, which courses between the popliteus muscle and the posterior surface of the tibia (aberrant anterior tibial artery), consistent with an anatomical variant.\n\nThe presence of a sesamoid bone, os fabella, is noted in the tendon of the lateral head of the gastrocnemius muscle, posterior to the corresponding femoral condyle.
MRI OF INTERNAL AUDITORY CANALS\n\nTECHNIQUE: Brain images were obtained using a 3T MRI scanner in axial, sagittal, and coronal planes with T1-weighted sequences, axial T2-weighted sequences, and axial T2-weighted sequences at the level of the internal auditory canals.\n\nDIAGNOSIS:\nNo evident pathology is documented in the anatomical structures of the inner ear on either side. The internal auditory canals are within normal limits, and the 7th and 8th cranial nerves show no apparent pathology. An ectopic vascular formation is noted above the right 7th and 8th cranial nerves (AICA vascular loop type 2), considered an anatomical variant, which requires clinical correlation.\n\nNo obvious pathological signal foci are identified in the cerebral hemispheres, brainstem, or cerebellum within the limits of the examination. The peripheral subarachnoid space, ventricular system, and cisterns appear normal for the patient's age, with no signs of compression, displacement, or abnormal dilation. A cavum septi pellucidi is noted as an anatomical variant.\n\nThickened mucosal changes are observed in the maxillary sinuses, particularly on the right, suggestive of chronic sinusitis. No pathological findings are noted in the anatomical structures of the skull base and mastoid air cells.
MRI Lumbosacral Spine\n\nTECHNIQUE: The examination was performed using T1, T2, and STIR pulse sequences in axial and sagittal planes.\n\nFINDINGS:\nThere is degeneration and loss of height of the intervertebral disc with a posterior central extruded herniation and caudal migration of disc material by 8mm at the L5-S1 level, displacing the right exiting L5 nerve root cranially. No significant central spinal stenosis is observed.\n\nMild degeneration with annular tear and circumferential bulging of the intervertebral discs is noted at the L3-L4 and L4-L5 levels, without compression of the neural structures. The presence of small perineural cysts is noted in contact with the left exiting L3 and L4 nerve roots.\n\nNo significant pathological findings are observed in the examination of the remaining intervertebral spaces.\n\nThere is also mild depression of the superior endplate of the L2 vertebral body.\n\nThe conus medullaris and cauda equina are in normal position and signal intensity.\n\nThe bone marrow shows normal signal intensity.
PITUITARY MRI\n\nTECHNIQUE:\nTransverse, sagittal, and coronal slices were obtained in T1 and T2 sequences, both before and after intravenous administration of contrast material. Thin slices of 3 mm were then taken in T1 sequence at the sella turcica in coronal and sagittal planes.\n\nDIAGNOSIS:\nWe have a previous pituitary MRI (04/12/2022) available for comparison. There is a noted reduction in the size of the previously identified heterogeneous lesion located in the anatomical position of the pituitary gland, to the left of the midline, measuring 0.43 x 0.37 x 1 cm (transverse x anteroposterior x craniocaudal dimensions), compared to the previous dimensions of 1.1 x 1.21 x 1.33 cm, likely associated with the presence of a macroadenoma. There is also moderate protrusion of the lesion into the left part of the sphenoid sinus, with an impression on the floor of the sella turcica, and extension into the left part of the suprasellar cistern. The lesion is in direct and broad anatomical contact with the left internal carotid artery, involving 1/4 of its circumference, accompanied by displacement outward and upward, without apparent reduction in its caliber or disturbance in its blood flow, a finding that does not substantiate infiltration of the left cavernous sinus.\n\n- In the current examination, there is noted heterogeneity of the pituitary gland corresponding to its right part, which was not previously present, without clearly defined focal lesion.\n\nThese findings require clinical and laboratory correlation and reevaluation in a subsequent imaging study. \n\nNormal imaging of the optic chiasm and right cavernous sinus is noted. The pituitary stalk is midline. Normal imaging of the paranasal sinuses and the anatomical structures of the skull base is noted. There is minimal fluid material in the right mastoid air cells.
MRI OF THE RIGHT ANKLE\n\nTECHNIQUE: The examination was performed using a 3 Tesla MRI system. Sequences included T1, T2, and PD with and without fat suppression techniques.\n\nFINDINGS:\n- A small amount of fluid is noted in the ankle joint.\n- There are small areas of bone marrow edema in the anterior part of the navicular bone, at the joint level between it and the lateral cuneiform, in the cuboid, in the proximal 4th and 5th metatarsals, in the talus, and in the calcaneus, likely of degenerative origin.\n- Subarticular cysts are observed at the proximal end of the 3rd metatarsal.\n- Findings consistent with tenosynovitis of the flexor tendons, primarily the posterior tibial tendon, are noted.\n- Mild paratenonitis and insertional tendinopathy of the Achilles tendon are present.\n- The plantar fascia, major ligamentous structures, and extensor tendons appear normal.\n- An accessory ossicle of the os trigonum type is present, considered an anatomical variant.\n- There is a significant amount of subcutaneous edema at the level of the medial malleolus and to a lesser extent on the plantar and dorsal surfaces of the foot.
MRI OF THE LEFT HIP\n\nTECHNIQUE: The examination was performed using a 3 Tesla MRI system. Sequences included T1, T2, and T2* with and without fat suppression techniques.\n\nFINDINGS:\n- Previous examination from 03/21/2024 is available for comparison.\n- There is worsening bone marrow edema involving most of the femoral head (except for the medial portion) and the neck of the left femur. No evidence of osteonecrosis, fracture, or deformity of the femoral head is observed.\n- Persistent bone marrow edema is noted in the left acetabulum, primarily at the roof, with no significant change from the previous examination.\nThese findings are primarily attributed to transient osteoporosis of the hip syndrome.\n- A small amount of joint effusion is again noted in the left hip joint, accompanied by synovitis and a small leaf-like lipoma in the anterior synovium. No pathology is observed in the articular cartilage of the femoral head.\n- No pathological findings are identified in the examination of the right hip.\n- Mild tendinopathy is noted at the insertion of the right hamstring tendons at the ischial tuberosity.\n- Minor degenerative osteoarthritic changes are observed at the level of the pubic symphysis.
MRI OF THE LEFT SHOULDER\n\nTECHNIQUE: The examination was performed using a 3 Tesla MRI system. T1 and PD/fs sequences were obtained in three planes.\n\nFINDINGS:\n- Mild degenerative changes are noted in the acromioclavicular joint, accompanied by limited subacromial bursitis.\n- There is evidence of degenerative tendinopathy of the supraspinatus tendon with a small partial-thickness tear on its bursal surface, located 1.2 cm from its insertion, extending intratendinously towards the myotendinous junction, with a total length of 2.5 cm.\n- Degenerative tendinopathy and a small partial-thickness tear are also noted at the insertion of the infraspinatus tendon (Rim-rent tear). There is thickening and degenerative tendinopathy of the subscapularis tendon, without a clear tear.\n- No significant fatty atrophy is observed in the rotator cuff muscles.\n- The long head of the biceps tendon is intact within its groove.\n- No abnormalities are noted in the articular cartilage or labrum.\n- There is blurring of the fat in the rotator interval, a finding that warrants clinical correlation for possible adhesive capsulitis.
MRI OF THE RIGHT KNEE\n\nTECHNIQUE: The examination was performed using PD FS and T1 sequences in axial, sagittal, and coronal planes.\n\nFINDINGS:\nThere is the presence of motion artifacts, which reduce the diagnostic clarity of the examination. Within the limits of the available evaluation:\n- No intra-articular fluid collection is observed.\n- The menisci, as well as the cruciate and collateral ligaments, appear normal.\n- The articular cartilage is within normal limits.\n- Bone marrow edema is noted in the lateral aspect of the lateral femoral condyle, suggesting a previous patellar dislocation. However, the bone marrow of the patella and the retinacular ligaments appear normal. Correlation with the history of injury is advised. Measurements of the extensor mechanism overlap show a normal TT-TG index of 1.2 cm, while there is a degree of hypoplasia of the medial femoral condyle and a shallow femoral trochlea of approximately 150 degrees.\n- A small cortical fibrous defect is identified on the posterior cortex of the femur.\n- The remaining bone marrow signal is within normal limits.
MRI OF THE RIGHT SHOULDER\n\nTECHNIQUE: The examination was performed using a 3 Tesla MRI system. T1 and PD/fs sequences were obtained in three planes.\n\nFINDINGS:\n- Moderate osteoarthritic changes are identified at the acromioclavicular joint.\n- There is a full/near-full thickness tear of the supraspinatus tendon with retraction of approximately 1.5 cm from its insertion. The possibility of a few intact fibers cannot be excluded.\n- Tendinopathy of the subscapularis is observed, with small intrasubstance tears near its insertion.\n- Significant fatty atrophy of the supraspinatus muscle is present (Goutallier grade IV), along with mild fatty atrophy (Goutallier grade II) of the other rotator cuff muscles.\n- The long head of the biceps tendon is located within its groove, with findings of tendinopathy and a tear in its intra-articular portion.\n- Small subcortical cystic changes are noted at the level of the lesser and greater tuberosities, consistent with rotator cuff tendinopathy.\n- A moderate amount of intra-articular fluid is present. No pathology is detected in the articular cartilage of the humeral head and glenoid.\n- There is a small area of subacromial bursitis.\n- Two areas of increased signal intensity are identified in the deltoid muscle near the acromioclavicular joint, likely related to the injection reported 10 days ago.
SACRUM MRI\n\nTECHNIQUE: The examination was performed using a 3 Tesla MRI system. Sequences included T1, T2, and STIR-weighted images.\n\nFINDINGS:\nWe have a previous sacrum MRI from 05/24/2022 for comparison.\n- The known lesion in the body of the L1 vertebra is re-evaluated, showing increased signal intensity on T2/STIR and low signal intensity on T1 sequence, measuring 1.88 cm x 2.13 cm in the transverse plane and 1.8 cm in the sagittal plane, compared to 1.92 x 2.12 x 1.94 cm previously (no significant change), consistent with a benign notochordal tumor.\n- The lesion on the right lateral aspect of the L4 vertebral body is re-evaluated, showing no change in imaging characteristics (mildly increased signal intensity on T2 and STIR sequences) and size (~1 cm), likely representing an atypical hemangioma.\n- A typical hemangioma measuring 0.7 cm in the L2 vertebral body is re-evaluated.\n- No pathological findings were observed in the remaining bone marrow, the visualized spinal cord, or the conus medullaris.\n- The alignment of the bony structures appears normal.\n- No evidence of spinal stenosis or foraminal narrowing was observed.\n- No pathology was detected in the paraspinal soft tissues.\n\nCONCLUSION: No significant change from the previous examination.

No significant signal alterations of the bony segments included in the various scan sequences.

There is the presence of fluid accumulation between the loops of the small intestine and in the pelvic cavity.

The findings are highly suggestive of an active inflammatory process (uncomplicated diverticulitis).

There is an associated predominantly hypoechoic and swollen appearance of the tail and body of the ipsilateral epididymis, with increased vascularity on color Doppler, increased velocity, and reduced resistive index on spectrometric analysis. There is a moderate fluid-particle hydrocele with focal thickening of the scrotal wall on the same side. The left testicle and epididymis are normal, with regular size, echostructure, and vascularization. The findings are consistent with acute orchiepididymitis.

In the scrotal area, likely in the epididymal region, multiple cystic formations appear to be recognizable, the largest about 1 cm, warranting specific ultrasound study.  \nCONCLUSIONS: Picture of benign prostatic hyperplasia. Possible epididymal cysts, to be monitored via ultrasound.

The calyceal-pelvic excretory cavities are not dilated bilaterally. No significant retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. Conclusions: Noted expansive formation in the right paramedian mesogastric region, with an ambiguous interpretation (GIST? retroperitoneal formation?). Indication: Evaluation in a patient with acute necrotizing hemorrhagic pancreatitis. MRI study acquired with axial and coronal scans using T2-weighted, DWI, and T1 sequences, the latter also repeated after selective fat signal saturation and intravenous administration of paramagnetic contrast agent (Gadoteridol), completed with MR sequence. The examination is technically limited by respiratory motion artifacts. With these limitations: previous CT reviewed. The pancreatic bed is completely replaced by a collection approximately 14 x 7.5 x 12.5 cm with irregular borders, characterized by heterogeneous signal due to the presence of fluid component, necrotic debris, and hemorrhagic component, with the collection extending to the lesser sac, root of the mesentery, peri- and para-duodenal region, retroperitoneum, and right and left peri- and para-renal spaces, at the latter level also with confluent micronodular consolidation. In today's examination, this collection also encases the celiac trunk, splenic vascular pedicle, and spleno-mesenteric-portal axis, with the splenic vein, superior mesenteric vein, portal vein, and its branches significantly narrowed. The left intrahepatic bile ducts, common hepatic duct, and proximal common bile duct are markedly dilated; the latter is notably narrowed in the intrapancreatic region; at this level, the walls of the common bile duct appear collapsed and markedly hypervascularized. There is likely an anatomical variant with biliary branches for the posterior segments of the right hepatic lobe that seem to converge into the left hepatic duct. The gallbladder is not visualized, likely due to previous cholecystectomy. Follow-up with CT is necessary. Conclusions: Extensive findings of acute necrotizing hemorrhagic pancreatitis with heterogeneous collections extending from the pancreatic bed to the lesser sac, retroperitoneum, peri- and para-renal spaces, peri- and para-duodenal region, and root of the mesentery, encasing contiguous vascular structures and causing marked stenosis of the spleno-mesenteric-portal axis.

CARDIAC MRI WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST\n\nThe examination was performed with the patient at rest, using Cine b-SSFP sequences aligned with the main cardiac axes, STIR sequences, and MDE sequences acquired after intravenous injection of Gadovist contrast agent, at a dose of 0.1 mmol/kg. Biometric data: height: 172 cm; weight: 100.5 kg; body surface area/BSA: 2.19 m² (according to Mosteller).\n\nClinical question: Further evaluation in a patient with a history of myocardial infarction (2012) treated with CABG and chronic heart failure with reduced ejection fraction.\n\nLeft Ventricle: Dilated and pseudo-aneurysmal appearance of the mid-apical segments, with thinning of the myocardial wall at the apex. Moderate reduction in global systolic function at rest (EF: 37%) with dyskinesia of the entire apex and hypo-akinesia of the anterior and septal segments at the mid-ventricular and apical levels, and lateral segment at the basal and mid-ventricular levels. Mild hypertrophy of the interventricular septum (14 mm).\n\nLeft Atrium: Slightly enlarged chamber dimensions, with mild mitral regurgitation (+/++++).\n\nRight Ventricle: Normal chamber dimensions, with preserved wall thickness, and moderate reduction in global systolic function at rest.\n\nRight Atrium: Slightly enlarged dimensions, with mild tricuspid regurgitation (+/++++).\n\nSTIR sequences do not reveal areas of altered intra-myocardial signal. Blood stasis phenomena are noted in the endo-cavitary apical region.\n\nPost-contrast study: Extensive confluent areas of late contrast enhancement are observed in the dyskinetic and hypo-akinetic segments described above, predominantly with a sub-endocardial distribution pattern; similar areas are noted in the papillary muscles.\n\nConclusions: Dilated left ventricle with a pseudo-aneurysmal appearance and reduced global systolic function at rest (EF: 37%); dyskinesia of the entire apex and hypo-akinesia of the anterior and septal segments at the mid-ventricular and apical levels, and the lateral segment at the basal and mid-ventricular levels. Extensive areas of myocardial necrosis are noted at the same levels, predominantly with a sub-endocardial distribution pattern in the dyskinetic and hypo-akinetic myocardial segments described above, as well as in the papillary muscles.

CARDIAC MRI WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST\n\nThe examination was performed with the patient at rest, utilizing cine and morphological sequences aligned with the main cardiac axes, STIR sequences, and PSIR sequences acquired after intravenous contrast administration in a patient with recent oppressive chest pain, elevated troponin levels, and suspected myocarditis.\n\nLeft Ventricle: Normal endocavitary dimensions with a diastolic diameter of 48 mm; preserved systolic function at rest with an overall ejection fraction of 64%.\n\nLeft Atrium: Normal dimensions, with no mitral insufficiency.\n\nRight Ventricle: Normal morphology and volume with preserved global systolic function at rest.\n\nRight Atrium: Mildly enlarged dimensions, with slight tricuspid insufficiency.\n\nSTIR Sequences: An area of inflammation and edema is noted in the myocardium, specifically in the lateral wall of the left ventricle at the basal and mid-ventricular levels, with an epicardial and sub-epicardial segmental distribution.\n\nPost-contrast Sequences: A faint area of mild enhancement is observed in the lateral wall of the left ventricle at the basal and mid-ventricular levels.\n\nConclusions: Preserved global left ventricular systolic function at rest (64%), with evidence of an area of myocardial inflammation and edema, with epicardial and sub-epicardial segmental distribution, in the lateral wall of the left ventricle at the basal and mid-ventricular levels, consistent with subacute myocarditis.
CARDIAC MRI WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST\n\nThe examination was performed with the patient at rest, using Cine b-SSFP sequences aligned with the main cardiac axes, STIR sequences, and MDE sequences acquired after intravenous injection of Gadovist contrast agent at a dose of 0.1 mmol/kg. Biometric data: height: 172 cm; weight: 78 kg; body surface area/BSA: 1.85 m² (according to Mosteller).\n\nClinical question: Further evaluation in a patient with acute coronary syndrome (ACS) with stenosis of the left anterior descending (LAD) coronary artery, treated with percutaneous angioplasty.\n\nLeft Ventricle: Enlarged cavity dimensions (maximum diameter 62 mm) with a pseudo-aneurysmal appearance of the apex, which shows thinned walls, and mild hypertrophy of the interventricular septum (13 mm). Severe reduction in global systolic function at rest (ejection fraction: 25%) with dyskinesia of the apex, akinesia of the anterior, anteroseptal, and inferior segments at the mid-ventricular level, hypo/akinesia of the mid-ventricular lateral wall, and hypokinesia of the inferior segment at the basal level.\n\nLeft Atrium: Cavity dimensions are within normal limits. Mild mitral regurgitation (+/++++).\n\nRight Ventricle: Cavity dimensions are within normal limits with preserved wall thickness, maintaining global and segmental systolic function at rest.\n\nRight Atrium: Dimensions are within normal limits. Mild tricuspid regurgitation (+/++++).\n\nSTIR sequences do not reveal any areas of altered intramyocardial signal.\n\nPost-contrast study: Presence of an extensive area of transmural necrosis at the apical and mid-ventricular levels, with relative sparing of the anterolateral segment and the mid-ventricular septum, as well as the inferior wall at the basal level.\n\nConclusions: Post-ischemic dilated cardiomyopathy with a pseudo-aneurysmal appearance of the apex, showing thinned walls and severe reduction in global systolic function at rest (ejection fraction: 25%). Accompanied by dyskinesia of the apex, akinesia of the anterior, anteroseptal, and inferior segments at the mid-ventricular level, hypo/akinesia of the mid-ventricular lateral wall, and hypokinesia of the inferior segment at the basal level, with myocardial necrosis in the same areas.
LUMBOSACRAL SPINE MRI\n\nIndication and clinical notes: trauma approximately 40 days ago.\n\nSequences: sagittal TSE T1, T2, and T2 Fat Sat, axial TSE T2.\n\nStraightening of the normal lordosis in the supine position.\n\nDepression of the middle-anterior third of the superior endplate of L1, with intraspongious bone edema, consistent with an unhealed vertebral fracture; neurosurgical/interventional neuroradiological consultation is necessary. Intraspongious edema involving the first coccygeal vertebra, which is reduced in height, consistent with a recent fracture, with periosteal edema.\n\nManifestations of lumbar spondyloarthritis with bridging marginal osteophytes (prominent at L5-S1) and hypertrophy of the facet joints.\n\nThe spinal canal is of preserved wiRXh in the explored segment.\n\nDegenerative changes of the intervertebral discs, with reduced height of the corresponding space at L5-S1.\n\nAt the D12-L1 level, there is a mild posterior median and bilateral paramedian disc protrusion that slightly indents the dural sac.\n\nAt the L1-L2, L2-L3 levels, there is a posterior median, paramedian, and bilateral sub-articular disc protrusion that indents the dural sac.\n\nAt the L3-L4 and L4-L5 levels, there is a broad-based posterior disc protrusion that indents the dural sac and partially occupies the neural foramina, particularly on the right.\n\nAt the L5-S1 level, there is a modest posterior median-paramedian bilateral disc protrusion that slightly indents the dural sac.\n\nThe conus medullaris is normally represented, terminating at the level of L1.

The spinal canal is of normal wiRXh.\n\nThere are early signs of dehydration in the intervertebral discs.\n\nAt the C3-C4 level, there is a minimal posterior median disc-osteophyte protrusion that slightly indents the anterior perimedullary space.\n\nAt the C4-C5 level, there is a posterior median disc-osteophyte protrusion that compresses the anterior perimedullary space.\n\nAt the C5-C6 level, there is a broad circumferential posterior disc-osteophyte protrusion that compresses the anterior perimedullary space, comes into contact with the ventral surface of the spinal cord, and partially encroaches on the neural foramina.\n\nThe spinal cord is normally represented in the explored segment.
LUMBOSACRAL SPINE MRI\n\nIndication and clinical notes: bilateral sciatica.\n\nSequences: sagittal TSE T1, T2, and T2 Fat Sat, axial TSE T2.\n\nStraightening of the normal lordosis in the supine position.\n\nFindings of lumbar spondyloarthritis with marginal osteophyte formation predominantly anteriorly, and hypertrophy of the facet joints and ligamenta flava.\n\nThe spinal canal maintains normal wiRXh in the examined segment.\n\nWidespread degenerative changes of the intervertebral discs (with reduced height of the L2-L3 space) and dystrophic changes of the opposing vertebral endplates, where small Schmorl's nodes are present.\n\nAt the L1-L2 level, there is a posterior paramedian, subarticular, and foraminal left disc herniation compressing the dural sac and occupying the corresponding neural foramen.\n\nAt the L2-L3 level, there is a posterior paramedian, subarticular, and bilateral foraminal disc protrusion indenting the dural sac and partially occupying the corresponding neural foramina.\n\nAt the L3-L4 level, there is a posterior median and bilateral paramedian disc protrusion indenting the dural sac.\n\nAt the L4-L5 level, there is a posterior median, paramedian, and bilateral subarticular disc protrusion indenting the dural sac and protruding into the corresponding neural foramina.\n\nAt the L5-S1 level, there is a broad-based posterior disc protrusion indenting the dural sac and occupying the corresponding neural foramina, particularly on the right, with possible conflict with the emerging L5 nerve root.\n\nThe conus medullaris is normally represented, terminating at the L1-L2 level.
RIGHT SHOULDER MRI\n\nIndication and clinical notes: painful shoulder in a patient who underwent rotator cuff repair approximately 12 years ago. Previous MRI from 11/29/22 reviewed.\n\nSequences: axial TSE PD SPAIR Fat Sat, coronal TSE PD and T2 SPAIR Fat Sat, sagittal TSE T1 and T2.\n\nThere are persistent degenerative arthritic changes in the acromioclavicular joint with remodeling of the joint surfaces and mild synovial thickening. The subacromial space is essentially preserved. There are post-surgical changes from rotator cuff repair, with significant tendinosis of the supraspinatus and infraspinatus tendons, which appear thinned and delaminated, with extensive areas of intratendinous degeneration but no clear signs of a tear. There is a slight reduction in the muscle bulk. Severe eccentric glenohumeral osteoarthritis is noted, with complete wear of the articular cartilage and friction phenomena of the opposing joint surfaces, currently with increased subchondral bone stress; there is a comparable inferior humeral polar osteophyte partially occupying the axillary recess. A small subchondral geode-cystic formation is present at the posterosuperior aspect of the humeral head, slightly increased in size. There is moderate joint effusion with distension of the periarticular recesses; minimal fluid distension of the subacromial-subdeltoid bursa. The long head of the biceps tendon, regularly contained within its groove, shows mild fluid distension of its sheath.

MRI of the Achilles Tendon\n\nA spindle-shaped thickening of the Achilles tendon was already suggested on the X-ray. In the MRI, the short tendon diameter at the point of maximum thickening measures approximately 14 mm. The tendon substance shows signal-rich, mild alterations in TIRM, T2 FS, and T1 sequences, with the tendon surface and insertion remaining intact. There is no local bursitis or fluid collection. There are pronounced edematous infiltrations in the subcutaneous fat tissue, as well as edematous deposits in the Kager's fat pad and slightly edematous areas in the muscles, such as the flexor hallucis longus. There is a mild, patchy alteration of the bone marrow, consistent with immobilization, but no fracture line. The joints are congruent with only minimal fluid, and there is no evidence of arthritis. The plantar fascia and short flexor muscles are normal. The extensors are unremarkable. There is no damage to the peroneal tendons. The interosseous membrane and syndesmotic ligaments are normal, and the fibulotarsal ligaments are continuous. In the available planes, no discernible damage to the deltoid ligament complex is observed.\n\nAssessment:\n- Tendinosis of the Achilles tendon, with relatively minor signal alteration, no tear, and no acute bursitis.\n- Edematous infiltrations in the subcutaneous fat and Kager's fat (clinically likely "edema").\n- Suspected immobilization osteoporosis, differential diagnosis includes medication-induced effects without insufficiency fracture.

The aorta remains well-opacified without significant stenosis.\n\nLeft Axis: The internal iliac artery (IIA) shows multiple stenoses up to 75%. There is a 50-75% stenosis at the origin of the external iliac artery (EIA), which is occluded from its origin. The SFA is narrow with multiple caliber variations and stenoses up to 50%. No significant stenosis of the popliteal artery (PA). Shortly after its origin, the anterior tibial artery (ATA) shows coarse calcification and is only questionably opacified distally. As far as can be assessed with venous contrast, the posterior tibial artery (PTA) and peroneal artery (PA) are opacified well into the periphery.\n\nRight Axis: In the bypassed and still perfused pelvic axis, there is no significant stenosis of the IIA. There is a 25-50% stenosis at the origin of the EIA, with a filiform stenosis mainly in the distal segment. At least 50% stenosis of the CFA. Approximately 50% stenosis near the origin of the PFA. The proximal SFA is also about 75% stenosed up to the anastomosis with the bypass. No significant SFA stenosis distal to the anastomosis. The PA is continuously opacified. The intrahepatic bile ducts are not dilated. The gallbladder is contracted and free of stones. The spleen, kidneys, and adrenal glands appear normal. There are no pathologically enlarged lymph nodes in the abdomen or retroperitoneum. No free fluid is present. The visualized bowel segments are unremarkable. The major abdominal vessels show no stenosis or aneurysms.  \nConclusion: The imaging findings demonstrate a mass in the pancreatic head with MRI characteristics typical of ductal adenocarcinoma. The abrupt interruption of the pancreatic duct and dilation of the bile ducts support this diagnosis. A solid pseudopapillary neoplasm cannot be definitively excluded based on imaging but is considered less likely.

They are positioned in the pouch of Douglas, demonstrating the "Kissing Ovaries" sign, and contain multilocular hemorrhagic cystic structures with T1 Fat Sat hyperintensity, suggestive of bilateral ovarian endometriomas.  \nEndometriotic implant in the pouch of Douglas.  \nInfiltration of the anterior rectal wall.  \nIntact vagina and bladder.  \nNo ascites or lymphadenopathy.  \n\nCONCLUSION  \n\nMRI findings suggest uterine adenomyosis with bilateral ovarian endometriomas ("Kissing Ovaries"), endometriotic implant in the pouch of Douglas, and infiltration of the anterior rectal wall on a myomatous uterus.

MRI OF THE TEMPORAL BONE\n\nINDICATION\nPneumococcal meningitis in August 2023 complicated by vasculitis and post-intubation tracheal stenosis, with recent findings of profound severe hearing loss on the left and moderate on the right in the latest ABR. Indication for cochlear implantation.\n\nTECHNIQUE\nUnder general anesthesia, 3D CISS, axial T2, axial gradient echo T2, 3D FLAIR, diffusion, 3D TOF, 3D T1 SPACE sequences before and after gadolinium injection. Magnetom Vida 3T DOTAREM pre-filled syringe 15ml\n\nRESULTS\nRight temporal bone:\nNo loss of physiological fluid signal in the cochleo-labyrinthine structures nor pathological contrast enhancement. Cochlear nerve present and of normal morphology. No anomaly of the acoustic-facial bundle.\n\nLeft temporal bone:\nLoss of physiological fluid signal in the middle third of the lateral semicircular canal and the posterior part of the superior semicircular canal, without spontaneous intra-labyrinthine T1 hypersignal and pathological enhancement. No loss of fluid signal in the cochlea. Cochlear nerve present and of normal morphology. No anomaly of the acoustic-facial bundle.\n\nRegression of T2 and FLAIR hypersignals in the bi-frontal periventricular white matter and bilateral frontal subdural collections with persistence of slight widening of the peri-cerebral fluid spaces in the frontal regions. Stability of the right frontal hemosiderin deposit sequelae along the path of an old intracranial probe and in the left sylvian fissure. No detectable pathological enhancement. No recent or established cerebrovascular accident. Integrity and patency of the entire circle of Willis, without irregularity. Slight decrease in sequelae ventricular dilation, with a bi-frontal diameter of 30 versus 37 mm on the CT scan of 01/03/2024.\n\nPartial bilateral filling of the maxillary sinuses and ethmoid cells.\n\nKnown subglottic tracheal stenosis. Posterior-basal ventilatory disorders and partial atelectasis of the posterior segment of the right upper lobe at the field limit.\n\nCONCLUSION\nLoss of fluid signal in the middle third of the lateral semicircular canal and the posterior part of the superior semicircular canal of the left temporal bone, suggestive of ossifying labyrinthitis in the fibrous/ossifying phase (no enhancement). Cochlear nerves present and of normal morphology.

MRI STROKE\n\nINDICATION\nRight-sided hemibody deficit with sensory predominance.\n\nTECHNIQUE\nDiffusion sequences, axial FLAIR, axial T2 gradient echo, 3D TOF, ASL, and 3D T1 FAT SAT post-gadolinium injection.\nMagnetom Sola\n\nRESULTS\nNo evidence of cytotoxic edema. No slow flow in the arteries.\nNo pathological blood signal or detectable thrombus.\nThe circle of Willis is fully patent, with no irregularities or stenosis.\nSome punctate FLAIR hyperintensities in the deep white matter, consistent with early vascular leukoencephalopathy, should be correlated with cardiovascular risk factors. No asymmetry on the ASL perfusion map.\nNo pathological contrast enhancement. Good patency of the venous sinuses.\n\nCONCLUSION\nNo recent or established cerebrovascular accident.\nNo significant vascular lesion.
BRAIN MRI\n\nINDICATION\nRight upper limb paralysis since yesterday. Initial brain CT scan normal. Suspected stroke.\n\nTECHNIQUE\nAxial diffusion, FLAIR, T2*, 3D TOF sequences acquired.\n\nMagnetom Sola\n\nRESULTS\nMultiple small cytotoxic lesions in the cortical and cortico-subcortical regions with diffusion restriction in the left posterior watershed area, mostly showing FLAIR hyperintensity (some small lesions are difficult to assess on FLAIR due to motion artifacts).\nNo slow flow.\nNo hemorrhagic transformation or microbleeds.\nNo thrombus on T2*.\nNo morphological or flow abnormalities in the circle of Willis.\nFetal origin of the right posterior cerebral artery arising directly from the right posterior communicating artery.\nNon-specific FLAIR hyperintensities in the periventricular and semi-oval centers.\n\nCONCLUSION\nEstablished punctate ischemic strokes in the left watershed area.\nNo proximal occlusion of the circle of Willis. Further exploration with a CT angiogram of the supra-aortic trunks is necessary.\n\nThe physician in charge of the patient has been informed of the results by phone.
CEREBRAL MR ANGIOGRAPHY\n\nINDICATION\nSudden onset of right lower limb weakness associated with dysarthria.\n\nTECHNIQUE\nDiffusion tensor imaging with six directions, axial FLAIR, axial gradient echo T2, and 3D TOF.\nSIEMENS SPECTRA\n\nRESULTS\nLeft lenticulostriate cytotoxic edema corresponding on FLAIR, related to an established cerebral infarction in the territory of the left deep middle cerebral artery.\nA portion of cytotoxic edema extends along the corticospinal tract and the posterior limb of the internal capsule to the proximal part of the right cerebral peduncle, associated with Wallerian degeneration.\nNo slow flow in the arteries.\nNo detectable blood signal or thrombus.\nAneurysm of the anterior communicating complex measuring 5x4 mm in diameter.\nIntegrity and patency of the remainder of the circle of Willis.\n\nCONCLUSION\nEstablished ischemic stroke in the deep territory of the left middle cerebral artery.\nAneurysm of the anterior communicating complex measuring 5x4 mm in diameter.

BRAIN AND SPINAL MRI WITH CONTRAST\n\nINDICATION: Examination performed in the context of a right fronto-parietal acute subdural hematoma, with suspicion of inflicted trauma.\n\nTECHNIQUE: Examination conducted under general anesthesia, using sagittal T1, axial T2, axial gradient echo T2, SWI, 3D FLAIR, diffusion tensor imaging with 30 directions, 3D T1 inversion recovery, ASL perfusion, spectroscopy of the pre-optic area, splenium of the corpus callosum, and left parietal region, and 3D T1 post-gadolinium injection on the brain. Sagittal T1 sequences post-gadolinium injection and sagittal T2 STIR sequences of the entire spinal cord and coronal T2 STIR at the thoracolumbar level. Magnetom Vida 3T DOTAREM FL INJ 20ML 2.8 CC\n\nRESULTS: Regarding the cerebral parenchyma, there is a right hemispheric convexity subdural hematoma of low volume, measuring a maximum thickness of 4 mm in the axial plane, without significant mass effect. There may be a focal venous thrombosis at the vertex in the right frontal region appearing as a clear hyposignal on the SWI sequence, however, without signal alteration on FLAIR and T1 weighting, and without clear enhancement post-gadolinium injection. No hydrocephalus. No signs of brain herniation. No cytotoxic edema. Right hemispheric hypoperfusion on ASL mapping. There is a slightly prominent appearance of the pericerebral spaces adjacent to the left cerebral hemisphere, associated with a slightly prominent appearance of the lateral ventricles, particularly the right lateral ventricle and the third ventricle. No pathological intraparenchymal hemorrhagic signal. No abnormal peak visible on spectroscopy. No visible lactate accumulation. No pathological contrast enhancement. Good patency of the venous sinuses.\n\nRegarding the spinal canal, no visible signal anomaly at the level of the spinal cord. Atelectasis of the right upper lung lobe.\n\nCONCLUSION: Right fronto-parietal subdural hematoma with possible presence of focal bridging vein thrombosis in the frontal region at the vertex, however, without signal alteration on T1 and FLAIR weighting, thus somewhat atypical for bridging vein thrombosis. No other detectable traumatic lesions.
