CT CHEST\n\nTechnique: The examination was performed using a helical technique before and after the intravenous administration of iodinated contrast material.\n\nFindings:\nA pathologically enlarged lymph node is identified in the left retrotracheal area, measuring 1.4 cm in diameter, with a central hypodense necrotic region. Additionally, a pathologically enlarged lymph node is noted in the left paraesophageal area, measuring 3.2 x 2.5 cm, also with a central hypodense necrotic region.\n\nThere is dilation of the esophagus with a wiRXh of up to 3.2 cm, starting at the level of the T6 vertebra and extending to just above the gastroesophageal junction, with irregular circumferential thickening of its wall and luminal narrowing. Correlation with endoscopic findings is recommended.\n\nNo focal lesions suggestive of metastatic involvement are identified in the pulmonary parenchyma.\n\nNo pleural or pericardial effusion is observed.\n\nA few small lymph nodes, measuring a few millimeters in diameter, are noted in both axillary regions, which are not considered pathologically enlarged based on imaging criteria.\n\nNo clear evidence of metastatic involvement is seen in the bones of the thoracic cage.
CT SCAN OF THE UPPER AND LOWER ABDOMEN\n\nTechnique: The examination was performed using a helical technique before and after the intravenous administration of iodinated contrast material.\n\nFindings:\nIn the hepatogastric space and lesser omental sac, there are large, confluent, sub-rounded lesions with lobulated margins, measuring 9.6 x 10.2 x 9.2 cm in total. These lesions exhibit thickened walls, mild peripheral contrast enhancement, small linear calcifications, and a central hypodense area of necrosis.\n\nThe lesions are in broad contact with the lesser curvature of the stomach and the pylorus, with preservation of the fat planes. There is indistinctness of the borders with the inner margin of the liver, suggesting possible tissue infiltration, particularly at the level of the caudate lobe. Caudally, there is also contact with the head and body of the pancreas, with loss of the fat planes.\n\nThe lesions encircle the common hepatic artery, which remains patent. They exert significant compressive effects on the intrahepatic portion of the inferior vena cava, which remains patent, while a filling defect is noted in the extrahepatic portion of the portal vein, suggestive of neoplastic infiltration/thrombosis.\n\nThe liver appears normal in size and morphology. A small hypodense lesion, 7 mm in diameter, is noted on the subdiaphragmatic surface, which cannot be characterized with certainty due to its small size.\n\nThere is no dilation of the intrahepatic or extrahepatic bile ducts. No obvious focal lesions indicative of deep-seated abnormalities are seen in the other solid organs of the upper abdomen.\n\nNo pathologically enlarged lymph nodes are identified in the retroperitoneal space, iliac chains bilaterally, or the femoral-inguinal regions.\n\nA small fluid collection is noted in the pouch of Douglas.\n\nNo significant pathological findings are observed in the imaged anatomical structures of the lesser pelvis.

CT SCAN OF THE UPPER AND LOWER ABDOMEN\n\nTechnique: The examination was performed using a helical technique before and after intravenous administration of iodinated contrast material.\n\nDiagnosis:\n\nHistory of colon cancer.\nThe liver parenchyma study reveals numerous hypodense lesions approximately 1.5 cm in size, with some showing a tendency to coalesce, suggestive of metastatic deposits. No dilation of the intrahepatic or extrahepatic bile ducts is observed.\nThe gallbladder shows mild pericholecystic edema.\nAn eccentric filling defect is noted in the left main branch of the portal vein, suggestive of thrombosis.\nThe right kidney shows reduced parenchymal enhancement compared to the left, with a pig-tail catheter whose tip ends in the urinary bladder. Moderate dilation of the pelvicalyceal system is noted.\nNo obvious focal lesions indicative of metastatic deposits are seen in the other solid organs of the upper abdomen.\nNumerous pathologically enlarged lymph nodes are identified in the retroperitoneal space, some with punctate parenchymal calcifications. The largest is in the upper retroperitoneal space, measuring 4.4 x 3.7 cm, with others para-aortic on the left measuring 1.8 cm, along the common iliac chains on the left measuring 1.6 cm and on the right 1.6 cm, and in the external iliac chains on the left measuring 1.2 cm and on the right 1.4 cm. A few lymph nodes are noted in the femoral-inguinal regions bilaterally with a short diameter up to 8 mm.\nA filling defect is noted in the inferior vena cava below the renal veins and in the left common iliac vein, suggestive of thrombosis.\nA small fluid collection is noted in the subhepatic space, in the paracolic gutters, with associated mesenteric fat stranding and fluid infiltration of the paranephric and lateral conal fascia bilaterally.\nAt the level of the rectosigmoid junction, there is an abrupt circumferential thickening of the bowel wall over a length of approximately 5 cm, with associated luminal narrowing. Correlation with colonoscopy findings is recommended.\nNumerous lytic lesions are identified in the visualized bony structures, suggestive of metastatic deposits in the T9, T10 vertebrae, with the development of a large soft tissue mass extending into the spinal canal and compressing the spinal cord, and in the L3, L4, L5 vertebrae with pathological fractures and a large soft tissue mass corresponding to the right psoas muscle, suggestive of infiltration, as well as in the left iliac bone and sacrum.\nIncidental subsegmental atelectasis is noted in the lower lobes with associated pleural effusions.
CT ANGIOGRAPHY OF THE NECK AND BRAIN\n\nTECHNIQUE\nThe examination was performed using a helical CT technique before and after intravenous contrast administration during the arterial phase, with thin slices taken from the level of the upper mediastinum to the upper part of the skull.\n\nFINDINGS\nHistory of thoracic aortic dissection.\nPostoperative changes are evident in the ascending aorta with the presence of a surgical graft.\nThe ascending thoracic aorta measures 4.7 x 3.6 cm in cross-section.\nImmediately after the distal anastomosis of the graft in the proximal aortic arch, a wall dissection is evident, involving the origins of all three vessels of the upper mediastinum. The dissection continues along the descending thoracic aorta.\nIn the right common carotid artery, the re-entry point is identified approximately 2.7 cm from the carotid bulb. No significant lesions are noted at the origin of the right internal carotid artery. Its course is satisfactorily visualized up to the intracranial segment, with the presence of tortuosity.\nIn the left common carotid artery, a re-entry point is identified approximately 6 cm from the corresponding carotid bulb. However, a linear filling defect is noted in the carotid bulb during the arterial phase of the examination, which is unclear if it pertains to a flow disturbance of the contrast medium and requires further evaluation with ultrasound. Distally, the course of the left internal carotid artery is normal up to its intracranial segment.\nThe vertebral arteries originate from the true lumen without stenosis and with satisfactory opacification throughout their course.\nThe intracranial vessels are patent and without stenosis, occlusions, aneurysms, malformations, or other types of vascular lesions. The circle of Willis is satisfactorily visualized without variations.\nThe vertebrobasilar system is evaluated intracranially without significant pathological findings.

The examination was performed before and after the intravenous administration of contrast material using a pulmonary artery angiography protocol. No filling defects are observed in the trunk, main, and segmental branches of the pulmonary artery to suggest the presence of a pulmonary embolism. No pericardial or pleural effusion is present. No enlarged lymph nodes in the thorax. No suspicious bone lesions. Atheromatous changes in the aortic arch and descending aorta. Mild emphysematous changes in the lungs, particularly in the upper lobes. No suspicious bone lesion.
Absence of diffusion restriction or abnormal enhancement in the brain parenchyma. Midline structures are midline. A 7 mm lesion near the left frontal horn with peripheral signal drop on T2*, resembling a popcorn-like appearance - likely a cavernoma. No signs indicative of recent hemorrhage are observed. Multiple small foci of high FLAIR and T2 signal in the semioval centers, increased for age, without pathological enhancement or diffusion restriction - nonspecific type.
Degenerative changes in the lumbar spine with hypertrophy of the facet joints and thickening of the ligamenta flava. There is a right paracentral disc herniation at the L5-S1 level, which compresses the emerging right S1 nerve root. There is a first-degree posterior spondylolisthesis of L5 over S1. Normal alignment of the remaining vertebrae.
Sternotomy-sternodesis. Cardiothoracic ratio increased. Prominent interstitial markings - linear atelectatic elements in the lungs.
On the left side of the neck, at the site of the swelling, a homogeneous hyperechoic lesion was identified, without vascularization, which does not appear to originate from the thyroid gland, with a maximum diameter of 4 cm. Considering the age of the patient, the ultrasound findings are more consistent with a second branchial cleft cyst.
Atherosclerotic changes are noted in the walls of the common and internal-external carotid arteries, with plaques present measuring up to 17mm in thickness in the common carotid arteries bilaterally, which do not cause significant stenosis. No evidence of luminal narrowing of the vessels is observed. Normal waveforms, direction, and flow velocities are noted in the vessels examined.
Puncture of the right common femoral artery using the Seldinger technique. Selective catheterization of the internal and external carotid arteries and the vertebral arteries. A left frontal arteriovenous fistula is identified, supplied by the middle meningeal arteries bilaterally and drained by cortical veins.
Widening of the superior mediastinum with the presence of a silhouette sign at the right hilum, a finding consistent with pathology in the anterior mediastinum.
No evidence of adenoma is observed. The pituitary gland shows normal dimensions for the age of the patient. The stalk is maintained in the midline, and the pituitary fossa is intact. The optic nerves and the optic chiasm appear normal. No significant findings from the brain parenchyma.
No fluid levels are observed.
Bilateral central opacities and the presence of Kerley B lines at the lung bases, findings consistent with pulmonary edema.
Presence of air under the right hemidiaphragm.
Normal size, echogenicity, and cortical thickness of the kidneys. No dilation of the pelvicalyceal systems. Absence of nephrolithiasis.
At the point of maximum tenderness, a non-peristaltic, non-compressible, blind-ended loop is identified, originating from the cecum, likely representing the appendix. It has an outer diameter of 7mm and is surrounded by free fluid. These findings suggest inflammation of the appendix/appendicitis. A moderate amount of fluid is also noted in the pouch of Douglas.
Multiple lytic lesions without sclerotic margins.
Hypodense lesion in the cortex and white matter of the right frontal lobe without perilesional edema, consistent with characteristics of a chronic infarct. No intracranial hemorrhage was visualized. Ventricular system and subarachnoid space are normal. Hypoplasia of the right frontal sinus.

CECT ABDOMEN\n\nFINDINGS\n\nThere is fatty infiltration of the liver. A possible 5 mm cyst is noted subcapsularly in segment IVb. The gallbladder shows no significant findings, and there is no biliary duct dilation observed. The pancreas appears edematous without parenchymal enhancement deficit, accompanied by a significant peripancreatic fluid collection (edematous pancreatitis, CT severity index 2 - mild). Referral data: Request for CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis, patient post ischemic stroke in 2022, hypertension; currently hospitalized due to a drop in Hb to 6.4 g/dl; CKD in history; seeking neoplasm.\nThe chest CT was performed after intravenous administration of contrast medium; the abdominal and pelvic CT was performed before and after intravenous administration of contrast medium (multiphase).\nDescription of the examination:\nChest CT:\nFluid in both pleural cavities with a thickness of up to 3.0 cm on the right and 1.4 cm on the left.\nBilateral band-like and nodular consolidations with calcifications visible in the basal segments - primarily inflammatory/atelectatic-fibrotic changes.\nIn both lungs, areas of discreetly different density are visible - higher density peripherally - primarily in the context of congestive changes/possibly mosaic perfusion.\nThe pulmonary trunk is enlarged to 3.6 cm, the right pulmonary artery reaches 2.6 cm, and the left 2.4 cm.\nSigns of heart failure, enlargement of heart atria.\nLungs without changes suggesting primary neoplasm or metastatic changes.\nNumerous lymph nodes visible in the mediastinum, some enlarged to 1.2 cm in short axis. In the pulmonary hila and axillary regions, no pathological enlarged lymph nodes were found.\nThe thoracic aorta is not dilated, with calcified atherosclerotic plaques present.\nAbdominal and pelvic CT:\nThe walls of the esophagogastric junction are somewhat blurred, with probable thickening - the image is ambiguous - endoscopic verification indicated.\nThe cecum has segmentally irregularly slightly thickened walls up to 0.7 cm, with somewhat increased contrast enhancement, without discernible changes in the surrounding adipose tissue - early imaging follow-up/endoscopic verification indicated.\nIn both kidneys, numerous cortical cysts are visible, the largest located in the left kidney reaching 4.7 x 3.3 cm. A single cyst with a diameter of 1.0 cm in the vicinity of the upper pole of the right kidney has increased density in the native phase (approx. 40 HU) - primarily a complicated cyst. Another cyst in the right kidney in the midsection measuring about 1.2 cm is slightly heterogeneous, without discernible contrast enhancement - probably another complicated cyst.\nBesides, both kidneys, as well as the liver, biliary system, spleen, pancreas, and adrenal glands show no discernible pathological changes in the CT examination.\nThe urinary bladder is contracted on a Foley catheter - not assessable for meaningful evaluation.\nA small amount of fluid in the Douglas pouch.\nThe cervix appears slightly thickened - to be assessed gynecologically.\nQuite numerous diverticula in the proximal part of the sigmoid colon, without evident signs of inflammation.\nIn the abdominal and pelvic cavity, no enlarged pathological lymph nodes were found.\nThe abdominal aorta is patent, not dilated with primarily calcified atherosclerotic plaques present. The celiac trunk, SMA, LRA, RRA, IMA are patent, without significant stenosis.\nThe bony structures within the scope of the examination show no changes suspicious for metastasis.\nStatus post compression fracture of the Th12 vertebral body. Left convex spinal curvature. Degenerative changes in the examined segment of the spine, especially at L2/L3 - lower endplate erosions of L2 are visible.\nOn the border of the examination, varicose veins of the pubic mound are visible.\nFluid in the pleural cavities. Signs of heart failure. Enlarged mediastinal lymph nodes.\nUnclear image of the esophagogastric junction - to be verified by endoscopy. Slight irregular thickening of the wall of the cecum - early follow-up in CT/endoscopy assessment indicated. The cervix appears slightly thickened - to be assessed gynecologically.

CT Angiography of the Lower Limbs\n\nThe examination was performed with a slice thickness of 1.25 mm following the intravenous administration of 100 ml of Omnipaque 350 contrast agent, with no early complications.\n\nClinical Data:\nAtherosclerosis of the lower limbs.\n\nExamination Description:\nThe abdominal aorta, visualized from the level of the renal arteries, is not dilated, with calcified walls and the presence of non-calcified atherosclerotic plaques up to 3 mm thick. In the upper part, dimensions are up to 17.5x15.5 mm, and above the bifurcation, 14x14.5 mm.\n\nThere is circumferential calcification of the walls at the origin and initial segment of the right renal artery, with a luminal narrowing of approximately 40-45%. Moderate luminal narrowing at the origin of the left renal artery, about 50%.\n\nParietal atheromatous changes are also visible in the superior mesenteric artery trunk, without significant luminal narrowing.\n\nIn the left kidney, there are segmental defects and narrowing of the parenchyma, mainly on the anterior surface.\n\nThe inferior mesenteric artery is patent.\n\nThe right common iliac artery (CIA) and right external iliac artery (EIA) show no significant stenosis, despite segmental intramural calcifications.\n\nThe left CIA is patent in the initial approximately 8 mm segment, and further along, over a length of about 42 mm (measured along the curve), it is occluded. Just before the bifurcation, it becomes opacified. In the occluded segment, with circumferentially calcified walls, it is filled with thrombus, with segmental calcifications in the lumen.\n\nThe left EIA is patent, slightly narrower than the opposite side. The right measures up to 7.5-8 mm, the left up to 5.5-6 mm.\n\nBoth internal iliac arteries (IIA) have multiple calcified atherosclerotic changes in the walls, are patent, with numerous narrowings.\n\nBoth common femoral arteries (CFA) are patent with parietal atheromatous changes, partially calcified. The right measures up to 8 mm, the left up to 6 mm.\n\nBoth deep femoral arteries (DFA) are patent, with numerous intramural calcifications, segmentally moderately narrowing their lumen.\n\nThe right superficial femoral artery (SFA) has scattered atheromatous changes, with irregular luminal contours and numerous narrowings. The largest, concentric narrowing is about 70% over a segment of approximately 6 mm located 8 cm from its origin. A similar 70% short-segment narrowing is visible in its middle part in the adductor canal. Other numerous narrowings range between 50 and 60%.\n\nThe left SFA has scattered atherosclerotic changes, with multiple narrowings, patent up to 50%.\n\nBoth popliteal arteries (POPA) are patent, with segmental luminal irregularities and parietal atheromatous changes, without significant stenosis.\n\nThe left lower leg arteries have scattered atheromatous changes.\n\nThe left anterior tibial artery (ATA) is narrow, at places threadlike, and occluded in the distal segment. The dominant left posterior tibial and peroneal arteries partially provide collateral circulation to the left dorsalis pedis artery, which is only segmentally visible. The left plantar artery and its branches are opacified.\n\nNumerous, at places significant/critical, luminal narrowings of the right posterior tibial artery.\n\nOtherwise, on the right side, the patency of the lower leg arteries is maintained, with visible transition into the plantar and dorsalis pedis arteries, despite numerous atheromatous changes and luminal narrowings.\n\nDegenerative changes in the vertebral bodies and intervertebral joints of the L segment of the spine included in the examination, with reduced height of the intervertebral discs.\n\nConclusions:\nScattered atheromatous changes in the aorta, iliac vessels, and arteries of the lower limbs.\n\nSegmental occlusion of the left CIA.\n\nMultiple narrowings of both SFA, at places up to 70% on the right side.\n\nOtherwise, as described.

It enhances heterogeneously post-contrast, protruding beyond the kidney outline. It exceeds the capsule boundary, infiltrates the surrounding fat tissue, and the posterior layer of Gerota's fascia. Near the tumor, there is a pathological network of pericapsular vessels, which shows connection with the vessels supplying metastatic neoplastic changes in the chest. Ureters are unchanged. Normal appearance of the urinary bladder. The prostate gland is not enlarged, with periurethral calcifications. Seminal vesicles are not enlarged. The lumbar spine and bony elements of the pelvis show no metastatic changes.\n\nConclusions: Neoplastic tumor of the lower part of the left kidney, on CT characteristic of RCC. MRI of the brain with contrast.\n\nThe examination was performed using axial T2, axial T2 FLAIR, axial DWI B100+ADC, axial 3D T1, axial T2*, +C sagittal T2, +C axial 3D T1, +C sagittal T1 sequences.\n\nReferral information: suspicion of a past ischemic episode of the central nervous system - please review.\n\nCompared with the brain MRI from 11.04.2023.\n\nExamination description:\n\nIn the current study, there are areas of increased signal intensity in the medial parts of the thalami bilaterally on T2-weighted and FLAIR images, measuring up to 7 mm, showing clear features of restricted water molecule diffusion - progression compared to the previous study. These areas do not show signal enhancement after intravenous contrast administration. The appearance primarily corresponds to ischemic changes in the vascular territory of the anatomical variant of the Percheron artery in the subacute phase. Differential diagnosis should consider "top of the basilar" artery infarction, changes due to toxic or metabolic disorders (in this location, in the course of vitamin B1 deficiency, which is unlikely given the patient's age. Cases of encephalopathy caused by flavivirus infection are also described in the literature).\n\nOtherwise, the white and gray matter of the brain shows normal signal intensity.\n\nThe ventricular system is not displaced, not enlarged, slightly asymmetric in the lateral ventricles L>P.\n\nThe cerebral aqueduct and fourth ventricle are normal.\n\nSignal from the structures of the posterior cranial fossa is normal.\n\nNo other areas of restricted water molecule diffusion are observed.\n\nNo evidence of hemoglobin metabolites is detected.\n\nVenous sinuses are patent.\n\nMinor mucosal changes in the maxillary sinuses.\n\nConclusions:\n\nSymmetrical, bilateral areas of diffusion restriction and abnormal signals within the thalami - the appearance primarily corresponds to ischemic changes in the vascular territory of the anatomical variant of the Percheron artery in the subacute phase. Correlation of the radiological findings with the clinical history is necessary.

According to the EU-TIRADS-PL scale, the thyroid focal lesions do not require fine-needle aspiration cytology (FNAC).\n\nThe vascularization of the thyroid is normal. The parotid and submandibular glands appear normal.\n\nThe lymph nodes in the examined area are not enlarged.

CT of the Cervical and Thoracic Spine in Emergency Mode\n\nThe examination was performed with a slice thickness of 1.2 mm, using soft tissue and bone algorithms in the survey phase.\n\nClinical Data: The patient, with a history of cancer (post-thyroid cancer surgery), presented with severe spinal pain at the junction of the cervical and thoracic regions. Tenderness of the spinous processes from C7 to Th3 upon pressure. Increased tension of the paraspinal muscles. No improvement with previous treatments (steroids, tramadol, ketonal).\n\nExamination Description:\n\nThere is a slight S-shaped curvature/scoliosis of the cervical and thoracic spine, left-to-right.\n\nThe heights of the vertebral bodies in the cervical and thoracic regions, their alignment with each other, the skull base, and L1 are normal. No malignant bone remodeling is observed.\n\nDegenerative changes are noted on the edges of the vertebral bodies C5, C6, C7, and thoracic vertebrae, with the most pronounced changes from Th5 to Th12. Degenerative changes in the intervertebral joints in the cervical region: from C2 to C7 on the right side and from C3 to Th1 on the left side.\n\nThere are sharpened uncinate processes and moderately pronounced degenerative changes in the uncovertebral joints at the levels of C5/C6 and C6/C7. The posterior edges of the vertebral bodies C5, C6, C7 mildly protrude into the spinal canal. There is a reduction in the height of the intervertebral spaces C5/C6, C6/C7. Narrowed intervertebral foramina and nerve root canals at the level of C6/C7. At this level, there is a broad-based protrusion of the intervertebral disc. The anteroposterior dimension of the dural sac at this level is narrowed to approximately 7 mm. No significant narrowing of the dural sac or pathological changes in the examined section of the spine are observed.\n\nConclusions: Cervicothoracic spondylosis. Details are provided in the description.
CT of the Abdomen and Pelvis\n\nThe examination was performed with a slice thickness of 1.5 mm before and in two phases after intravenous administration (without early complications) of 80 ml of contrast agent Omnipaque 350.\n\nClinical Data: Crohn's disease, unspecified.\n\nExamination Description\n\nNo pathological changes were observed in the lower parts of the chest included in the scan.\n\nNo free gas is detected in the peritoneal or retroperitoneal space.\n\nIn segment 2 of the liver, there is a 3mm hypodense area of indeterminate character. Otherwise, the CT appearance of the liver, spleen, adrenal glands, pancreas, gallbladder, and kidneys is normal. Bile ducts are not dilated. Hepatic veins and branches of the portal system are patent. Ureters are not dilated, without calculi.\n\nThe urinary bladder is moderately filled with urine, without detectable changes. The appearance of the reproductive organs on CT is without pathological features. Major vessels of the abdomen and pelvis are not dilated. No enlarged lymph nodes are observed along the major vessels. Free fluid in the peritoneal cavity, with separation up to 8 mm in Morrison's pouch, and a larger amount of fluid interloop in the pelvis and lower abdomen.\n\nLoops of the small intestine (ileum and jejunum) are dilated, with fluid levels. Inflammatory infiltration with marked stratification of the walls of the terminal ileum involving the ileocecal valve area over a length of approximately 9 cm. The thickness of the altered wall is between 5 and 13mm, with significant narrowing of the intestinal lumen. Before the narrowing, the ileum is dilated up to 46mm with residual content mimicking the contents of the large intestine. Circumferential thickening of the walls up to about 11-12mm over a segment of approximately 4 cm in the mid-transverse colon, without clear stratification, with significant narrowing of the intestinal lumen. Circumferential thickening of the walls up to about 4-7mm at the descending-sigmoid junction and sigmoid colon without significant narrowing of the intestinal lumen.\n\nIschiorectal fossae are clear.\n\nThe skeletal system shows no malignant structural remodeling.\n\nConclusions: Inflammatory infiltration with marked stratification of the walls of the terminal ileum involving the ileocecal valve area, causing intestinal obstruction. Free fluid in the peritoneal cavity. Two-site circumferential thickening of the walls in the large intestine, with significant narrowing of the lumen in the mid-transverse colon. Otherwise, as described above.
CT angiography of the intracranial arteries was performed with a 0.625 mm slice thickness in the survey phase and two phases following the intravenous administration of 80 ml of Omnipaque 350 contrast agent, according to the brain death protocol.\n\nReferral information: Status post cranio-cerebral trauma. Status post cardiac arrest with asystole mechanism and successful resuscitation. Brain edema. Suspected brainstem herniation.\n\nExamination description:\n\nIn the first phase of contrast enhancement, contrast enhancement of the internal carotid arteries was observed up to the C7 level, with trace enhancement of the A1 segment of the left anterior cerebral artery and M1 segment of the left middle cerebral artery. The remaining intracranial arteries were not enhanced.\n\nIn the first phase, contrast enhancement of the external carotid and superficial temporal arteries was visible.\n\nNo enhancement of the deep cerebral veins or venous sinuses was observed.\n\nGeneralized brain edema with obliteration of the differentiation between gray and white matter both above and below the tentorium. Narrowing of the cerebral sulci and cerebellar fissures. Effacement of cerebral sulci with smoothing of the gyri.\n\nThe supratentorial ventricular system is not displaced, narrow, and symmetrical.\n\nConclusions:\n\nCriteria for cessation of cerebral circulation are met:\n\n1. Lack of enhancement of the cortical branches of the middle cerebral arteries in the second phase after contrast administration.\n\n2. Lack of enhancement of the internal cerebral veins in the second phase after contrast administration.\n\n3. Enhancement of the superficial temporal artery lumens in the first phase after contrast administration.\n\nOtherwise, as described.
CT Angiography of the brain was performed urgently after intravenous administration of 80ml of contrast agent, with 0.625mm slices, using the brain death protocol (arterial phase and after 40 seconds).\n\nReferral Information: Suspected brain death.\n\nDescription: Extensive generalized brain edema with loss of differentiation between gray and white matter both above and below the tentorium. Obliteration of the cerebrospinal fluid spaces and compression of the brain sulci.\n\nHemorrhagic focus in the left hemisphere of the brain, comparable to the CT from 07.02.22.\n\nA subdural hematoma along the left hemisphere with a separation of up to 7mm is visible.\n\nMidline structures are shifted approximately 12mm to the right. The supratentorial ventricular system is compressed, with blurred external contours. Compression of the left lateral ventricle and the third ventricle.\n\nThe cerebral aqueduct and the fourth ventricle are not visible.\n\nVolume reserve at the level of the foramen magnum is obliterated.\n\nTonsillar herniation into the foramen magnum.\n\nIn the contrast phases of the study:\n\nA vascular catheter is introduced into the right internal jugular vein (IJV).\n\nRight common carotid artery (CCA) diameter is 7mm, left CCA diameter is 6mm.\n\nBoth CCAs are properly contrasted, with no significant stenosis.\n\nIn the arterial phase of the study, the internal carotid arteries (ICAs) in the cervical segments are contrasted only in the initial segments, approximately 2cm in length.\n\nVertebral arteries (VA) up to the level of the C1 vertebra.\n\nThe branches of the external carotid arteries, including the superficial temporal and facial arteries, are properly contrasted.\n\nIn the arterial phase of the study, no contrast is observed in any of the intracranial arteries or dural sinuses.\n\nIn the second phase of the study, the intracranial arteries are not contrasted.\n\nThe dural venous sinuses and internal cerebral veins (ICV) are not contrasted. Thrombi are visible in the bulb of the right internal jugular vein and in the paravertebral venous plexuses.\n\nConclusions:\n\nExtensive brain edema with herniation into the foramen magnum. Hemorrhagic focus in the left hemisphere of the brain. Subdural bleeding on the left side.\n\nConditions 1, 2, and 3 for the diagnosis of cerebral circulatory arrest in CT angiography are met:\n\n1. Bilateral lack of contrast enhancement of the MCA-M4 branches in the second phase after contrast administration.\n\n2. Bilateral lack of contrast enhancement of the internal cerebral veins in the second phase after contrast administration.\n\n3. Visible contrast enhancement of the branches of the external carotid arteries.
HRCT of the Lungs\n\nThe examination was performed in a survey phase with continuous slices of 1.25 and 2.5 mm thickness during inspiration and expiration.\n\nReferral data: COPD, pulmonary fibrosis, assessment of changes.\n\nExamination Description:\n\nFeatures of lung hyperinflation. No emphysematous bullae, pan- and centrilobular emphysema, or paraseptal emphysema are observed. There are single fibrous streaks and bands of plate-like atelectasis in segments V, VIII, IX, and X of the right lung and segments V, VIII, and X of the left lung. No other changes in the lungs are noted.\n\nSegmental mild, cylindrical bronchiectasis in segment X of the lungs. No other changes in the trachea or bronchial tree are visualized.\n\nPleural cavities are free of fluid.\n\nNo enlarged lymph nodes are observed in the mediastinum, pulmonary hila, or axillary regions.\n\nThe heart is not enlarged.\n\nThe major mediastinal vessels are not dilated.\n\nThe visualized bony structures show no signs of malignant structural remodeling.\n\nModerately pronounced degenerative changes in the thoracic spine, with the presence of marginal vertebral body spurs, single osteophytes, and small defects near the endplates of the lower thoracic vertebrae.\n\nThe partially visualized upper abdominal organs show no signs of pathology.\n\nConclusions:\n\nFeatures of lung hyperinflation. In segment VI of the right lung, a multi-lobular area of panlobular emphysema. Single fibrous bands in both lungs.

Few diverticula in the sigmoid and descending colon, up to 6 mm in size.\n\nThe liver, in a single-phase study (in the survey and venous phases), is homogeneous, not enlarged, with a 5 mm cyst in segment 2.\n\nThe gallbladder, bile ducts, and biliary system are unchanged.\n\nHepatic veins are not dilated.\n\nThe spleen is homogeneous without heterogeneous changes. CT of the Paranasal Sinuses 02/04/2022\n\nThe examination was performed with slices of 1.2/0.6 mm thickness, using both soft tissue and bone algorithms in the survey phase, with secondary multiplanar reconstruction.\n\nExamination Description:\n\nThe paranasal sinuses are normally developed. The ethmoid roof is of Kerost type III.\n\nThe frontal and sphenoid sinuses are normally aerated on both sides, showing no abnormalities. Supraorbital ethmoid cells are present.\n\nThe ethmoid air cells are mostly aerated, without pathological features, except for segmental thickening of the mucous membrane.\n\nThe right maxillary sinus is completely non-aerated, filled with masses of heterogeneous fluid-tissue density (ranging from 8 to a maximum of 74 Hounsfield units) with an expansile growth pattern. The highest density of the mass is in the medial part of the alveolar recess. These masses protrude through the enlarged opening and defects in the medial wall of the sinus into the nasal cavity. The continuity of the medial bony wall of the sinus is difficult to trace. The sinus opening to the middle nasal meatus is widened to approximately 18mm (anterior-posterior). The remaining bony walls of the sinus are moderately expanded.\n\nTissue masses measuring approximately 38x10x43mm (cranio-caudal x superior-inferior x anterior-posterior) in the right nasal cavity completely fill the middle nasal meatus, causing displacement and partial atrophy/compression or destruction? of the middle turbinate. They almost completely occupy the superior nasal meatus and the medial part of the inferior nasal meatus. They also protrude into several ethmoid cells on the right in the middle part.\n\nOtherwise, the bony elements of the walls of the paranasal sinuses show no signs of pathological remodeling.\n\nThe left maxillary sinus contains several polypoid, smooth-contoured structures, with a density of about 25 Hounsfield units and a diameter up to 6mm near the sinus opening and up to 12mm in the alveolar recess.\n\nThe openings of the frontal and sphenoid sinuses and the left ostiomeatal complex are patent. The remaining sinus openings and the right ostiomeatal complex are occupied by tissue-fluid masses.\n\nPneumatization of the left nasal turbinate with partial involvement of the cavity by a mucosal thickening approximately 5mm in diameter. Otherwise, the nasal turbinates on the left side appear normal.\n\nThe nasal passages on the left side maintain patency.\n\nThe nasal septum is straight.\n\nTeeth 18 and 28 are unerupted.\n\nIn the greater wing of the sphenoid bone on the left side, there is sclerotic remodeling of the bone structure consistent with fibrous dysplasia, measuring approximately 21.5x11x14mm.\n\nConclusions: Pathological tissue-fluid masses in the right maxillary sinus and nasal cavity of indeterminate character. Differential diagnosis includes antrochoanal polyp, inverted papilloma, nerve sheath tumor, or mixed tumor.

The body of the pancreas is not enlarged, with a fairly smoothly dilated pancreatic duct up to 7 mm and small calcifications in the parenchyma. The upper part of the pancreatic head is heterogeneous and slightly hypodense over an area of approximately 20 x 18 mm, with small parenchymal calcifications. The rest of the pancreatic head is not enlarged, with numerous parenchymal/periductal calcifications. In the pancreatic head, the dilated pancreatic duct up to 7.5 mm contains stones, the largest being up to 8 mm in the periampullary segment.\n\nIn the medial wall of the duodenal bulb and the initial segment of the descending part of the duodenum, there are closely located fluid spaces of pseudocystic nature (with wall enhancement) measuring up to 25 mm and 18 mm on transverse scans. They may be connected by a narrow stalk. They narrow the intestinal lumen. There is no fat tissue separation from the adjacent pancreatic head. In their vicinity (medially and below), in the hepatoduodenal ligament, subdiaphragmatically, near the left adrenal gland, there are numerous homogeneous lymph nodes with a short axis from 6 to 13 mm - the largest near the mesenteric vessel branches in the mid-abdomen and behind the portal vein.\n\nThe celiac trunk, superior mesenteric artery and branches, portal vein with tributaries are patent, without luminal narrowing/obstruction and without signs of infiltration.\n\nSimple cyst of 29 mm in the left kidney. Two left renal arteries.\n\nOtherwise, the CT image of the abdominal organs (adrenals, kidneys, spleen, gastrointestinal tract) shows no pathological changes.\n\nThe skeletal system shows no pathological structural remodeling.\n\nConclusions:\n\nChronic calcifying pancreatitis (possibly due to periduodenal inflammation).\n\nHowever, due to the lack of history of previous episodes of pancreatitis and the presence of numerous lymph nodes, the coexistence of pancreatic cancer cannot be definitively excluded. Consultation at a referral center is recommended.

MRI of the brain.\n\nThe examination was performed with the administration of 8.5 ml of Gadovist 1 MOL intravenously.\n\nExamination description:\n\nWithin the right hemisphere of the brain, across all lobes, multiple foci are visible, showing diffusion restriction characteristics and elevated signals on FLAIR images. The largest ischemic areas are located in the right occipital lobe, measuring 23 x 31 mm (with visible post-contrast enhancement along the gyri), and in the right parietal lobe, measuring 35 x 14 mm; these changes are accompanied by narrowing of the sulci and grooves of the brain. The image suggests the presence of acute ischemic foci within the vascular territory of the distal branches of the left middle cerebral artery. Hyperintense areas are visible in the white matter of both hemispheres, located subcortically and periventricularly, consistent with neurodegenerative changes and leukoaraiosis. Adjacent to the body of the right lateral ventricle, there are a few signal void areas surrounded by a zone of gliotic scarring, corresponding to lacunar changes, the largest measuring up to approximately 7 mm. The supratentorial ventricular system is enlarged, non-displaced, symmetrical, with the third ventricle measuring 13 mm in wiRXh. No hemoglobin metabolites are detected in the brain parenchyma on gradient sequences. A more detailed assessment of the brain is recommended with a full contrast-enhanced study. No other changes are noted.\n\nConclusions:\n\nAcute ischemic foci within the vascular territory of the distal branches of the left middle cerebral artery.

There is a fusiform aneurysm of the abdominal aorta measuring up to 60x53mm, extending approximately 127mm, with a partial rupture of the anterior wall about 47mm above the aortic bifurcation. The average flow lumen within the aneurysm measures approximately 27x23mm. It begins just below the origin of the main right and left renal arteries and extends to the level of the aortic bifurcation. The left kidney is supplied by two renal arteries, with an additional left renal artery originating about 12mm above the main renal artery, supplying its lower pole. Both kidneys have cortical cysts, with the left side measuring up to 31x42mm. The right external iliac artery is significantly narrowed in its initial segment over a length of approximately 13mm.\n\nThe skeletal system shows no pathological destruction.\n\nConclusions:\n\nFusiform aneurysm of the abdominal aorta with partial rupture of its anterior wall. ULTRASOUND OF THE LEFT KNEE WITH ULTRASOUND-GUIDED BIOPSY\n \nClinical history: 20-year-old male with knee mass, for biopsy\n \nComparison: MRI dated 13 September 2024 \n \nFindings:\n \nLimited ultrasound evaluation of the anterior aspect of the left knee demonstrates a large intra-articular lesion at the region of Hoffa’s fat pad. The lesion measures approximately 6.5 x 9.0 x 5.2 cm and demonstrates a heterogenous echotexture and mild internal vascularity. There is a moderate knee joint effusion.\n \nProcedure: \n \nThe procedure and its potential risks (including bleeding, infection, and damage to surrounding structures) were explained to the patient and written informed consent was obtained.\n \nA medial approach was used, as requested by the referring orthopedic oncologist. \n \nUsing a sterile technique, local anesthesia with 1% lidocaine was administered. Under ultrasound guidance, an 18-gauge core biopsy system was advanced into the lesion. Five samples were obtained and sent to pathology. \n \nThe procedure was well tolerated without immediate complications.\n \nConclusion:\n \nAn ultrasound-guided biopsy of the intra-articular left knee mass was performed.

Clinical/Indication: Active B1 bleeding. Is coiling possible?\nFindings: Written consent was obtained at the time of the examination. Laboratory parameters were within the reference range. A 6 French sheath was exchanged using the Seldinger technique. A 5 French Pigtail catheter was used for DSA of the aortic arch. Selective catheterization of the ostia of the bronchial arteries was performed. No arterial bleeding was detected. Superselective catheterization with a microcatheter was insufficiently achieved. The sheath was secured, and a bandage was applied. After the examination, a bladder catheter was placed by colleagues in the urology department. Improvement in symptoms was observed following this procedure.\nAssessment: Primary uncomplicated visualization of the bronchial arteries was achieved. No active bleeding was detected. Due to the course of the bronchial arteries in the target area, embolization was technically not feasible. The examination was terminated due to the patient's increasing restlessness.\nRecommendation for the next procedure: The sheath should be left in place for now and removed within 24 hours at the latest.
Clinical Indication: HCC, pre-SIRT\n\nFindings: Written informed consent and signed approval were obtained at the time of intervention. Coagulation parameters, creatinine, and TSH are within normal limits. The patient was positioned supine on the examination table. Sterile precautions were taken in the right inguinal region. Clinical/Indication: The patient, at 16 weeks of gestation, has a history of a minor cesarean section following the development of placenta increta in the uterine scar area due to ectopic placentation. An operation was performed on 12/19/23. The patient presented again due to recurrent vaginal bleeding; suspicion of vascular malformation, and the possibility of embolization if necessary.\n\nFindings: The purpose, risks, and alternatives of the procedure were explained in advance. TSH, creatinine, and coagulation parameters are within normal ranges. Sublingual Tavor was administered prior to the procedure. The patient was positioned supine with the head elevated on the examination table. Extensive local anesthesia was applied to the puncture site. A retrograde puncture of the right CFA was performed using an Angio-needle. A 5F sheath was placed using the Seldinger technique. The aortic bifurcation was found at a high level. The left internal iliac artery was catheterized with a 5F-RIM catheter. DSA was performed from multiple angles. A high-flow vascular lesion was identified, showing multiple tortuous arterial feeders and enlarged venous drainage to both the left and right internal iliac arteries. A classic arteriovenous malformation nidus was not detected. Therefore, in the differential diagnosis, retained placenta or atypical AVM could be considered. There was no evidence of active bleeding. After the procedure, foreign material was removed, and the puncture site was sealed with a 5F-Exoseal. Safeguard was applied.\n\nAssessment: No evidence of active bleeding. The visualization of a high-flow vascular lesion is likely consistent with retained placenta, with atypical AVM considered in the differential diagnosis.\n\nRecommendations for subsequent procedures:\n- In the event of acute bleeding, referral to gynecology and hysterectomy is recommended.\n- Clinical follow-ups should be conducted. If clinically acceptable and the course is uneventful, an MRI for follow-up should be performed in 2-3 months; if suspicion of AVM persists during this period (if the vascular lesion does not regress), transarterial embolization may be considered.
Clinical Indication: Liver Chemoembolization (TACE), diagnosis of HCC, suspicion of recurrence following TACE on 15.09.2023, and recommendation by the tumor board for repeat TACE.\n\nFindings: The purpose, risks, and alternatives of the procedure were explained in advance. Creatinine, TSH, and coagulation parameters were within normal limits. A signed consent form was available at the time of the examination. Analgosedation was administered with 7.5 mg Piritramide, 1 g Metamizole, 10 mg Metoclopramide, and 4 mg Ondansetron intravenously. Sterile precautions were taken in the right inguinal region. Comprehensive local anesthesia was applied with 10 ml of 1% Mepivacaine. The right femoral artery was punctured using a Terumo needle under fluoroscopic guidance. A standard Terumo wire was placed, and a 4F short sheath (11 cm) was inserted using the Seldinger technique. 5000 IU of Heparin was administered intra-arterially. A 4F-C2 catheter was placed, and the SMA was probed, followed by DSA. The celiac trunk and common hepatic artery were probed. Hepatic angiography was performed. The right hepatic artery was probed, and DSA was conducted. Superselective probing of the Segment IV artery was performed, and a mixture of Embocept and Epirubicin was administered. Subsequently, the Segment VIII artery was probed, and the Embocept and Epirubicin mixture was delivered. A total of 100 mg of Epirubicin was administered. DSA was performed via the macrocatheter in the right hepatic artery. No retrograde flow was detected during the application of Spheres. Minimal residual contrast was observed in the peripheral part of the tumor from the treatment position in Segment VIII. Foreign material was removed, and manual compression was applied until hemostasis was achieved at the puncture site. The sulcal and cisural patterns are normal, and the differentiation between gray and white matter is usual.\nNo focal lesions are observed at the level of the semioval centers and radiating crowns. However, there is an observed bulging of the sella turcica, which leads to a decreased amplitude of the suprasellar cistern.\nThe optic chiasm has well-defined margins. The corpus callosum has normal development, with regular, well-defined contours and homogeneous parenchyma.\nThe ventricular system, as well as the supratentorial and infratentorial cisternal systems, maintains normal disposition, with preserved caliber.\nIn the infratentorial region in the posterior fossa, the development of the cerebellar lobes and brainstem is normal, with homogeneous signal intensity. The tentorium is preserved. There are no focal lesions in the pontocerebellar angles.\nIn the TOF MRA sequence, the caliber and course of the vascular structures forming the anterior and posterior circulation of the brain are normal. Absence of the right posterior communicating artery is noted.\nThe intraorbital structures, including the optic nerve and the oblique and rectus muscles, show normal signal intensity and disposition.\n \nRADIOLOGICAL IMPRESSION:\nBrain without evidence of structural and/or traumatic lesions at the time of the study.\nImage suggesting pituitary hypertrophy, probably of physiological origin.\nAbsence of the right posterior communicating artery.\nCorrelation with clinical context is suggested.

MRI OF THE BRAIN (SIMPLE)\n \nUsing multiple pulse sequences, images of the skull were obtained in coronal, sagittal, and axial planes, as well as 3D TOF (time-of-flight) angiography sequences with multiplanar reconstructions, revealing the following findings:\n \nIncreased amplitude of the subarachnoid space and ventricular system, especially in the left parietal region, due to a slight generalized decrease in cortical-subcortical cerebral and cerebellar volume, chronologically related to the patient.\nThe splenium of the corpus callosum shows hypointensity with diffuse borders, hyperintensity on T2 and FLAIR sequences, mild restriction on diffusion, and hyperintensity on attenuated diffusion coefficient sequences.\nFindings of indeterminate origin, with subacute ischemia in a branch of the posterior cerebral artery. Possible causes include infectious inflammatory process, post-traumatic, demyelinating, or ethanol toxicity.\nWide perivascular spaces in the semioval centers, basal ganglia, and periphery of the anterior commissure as incidental non-pathological findings. A cystic image of 9.4x6.3 mm to the right of the anterior commissure, which does not restrict diffusion, with indeterminate origin. A giant perivascular space is noted as an incidental finding, with differential diagnosis including lacunar ischemia or more remotely, vesicular cysticercosis.\nOn T2 and FLAIR sequences, fine hyperintensity in the periphery of the lateral ventricles and sparse (six) nodular hyperintensities ranging from 1.7 to 4.8 mm in subcortical frontoparietal regions, without restrictive changes on diffusion, possibly representing chronic microangiopathy, with atherosclerosis as the primary possibility.\nThe superficial and deep venous system shows no evidence of pathology.\nThe arteries are patent and tortuous.\nThe skull, pericranial tissues, the remainder of the cerebral hemispheres, gray-white matter interface, and other structures in the sellar region, posterior fossa, cranio-cervical junction, and intraorbital areas show no abnormalities.\nSlight deviation of the nasal septum in its turbinal portion with left convexity. Evidence of chronic rhinitis. Mild thickening (0.7 mm to 1 mm) of the mucosa covering some ethmoidal cells.\n \nDIAGNOSTIC IMPRESSION:\nSlight generalized decrease in cortical-subcortical cerebral and cerebellar volume.\nChanges in signal intensity in the splenium of the corpus callosum of indeterminate origin, possibly related to ischemia, infectious inflammatory process, post-traumatic, demyelinating, or ethanol toxicity. If clinically deemed appropriate, it is suggested to complement the study with intravenous contrast and spectroscopy of this area.\nSubcortical frontoparietal nodular hyperintensities, possibly sequelae of chronic microangiopathy, with atherosclerosis as the primary possibility.\nSlight deviation of the nasal septum in its turbinal portion with left convexity. Evidence of chronic rhinitis. Mild thickening in the mucosa covering some ethmoidal cells.

Procedure: Computed tomography of the abdomen from the diaphragmatic domes to the pubic symphysis, performed in both plain and contrast-enhanced phases, showing the following:\n \nBoth hemidiaphragms appear normal. The liver gland shows normal overall morphology, with regular contours and homogeneous density, without evidence of focal or diffuse lesions. There is an increase in the size of the left lobe and a qualitative decrease in the right lobe. The vascular structures and bile ducts appear normal. No focal or diffuse enhancements are observed.\nCollateral formations are identified at the lower esophageal level, at the gastroesophageal junction, splenorenal, and periportal areas, with enhancement after contrast medium administration and serpentine disposition, as well as recanalization of the falciform ligament with collateral formations at the anterior abdominal wall level.\nFree fluid with hypodense and homogeneous characteristics is identified, occupying all supra- and infra-mesocolic recesses and extending to the pelvic cavity, with centripetal displacement of intestinal loops.\nThe gallbladder is observed in its anatomical position, with a pear-shaped morphology, hypodense interior, homogeneous, with a hypodense halo around it.\nThe pancreas has a normal morphology, with regular contours and appropriate relation to its anatomical markers, the superior mesenteric vein, and the splenic vein, without alterations in its different segments (head, body, and tail).\nThe spleen shows increased size with a cranio-caudal length of 14.9 cm and preserved density, with no apparent focal pathology.\nThe kidneys are symmetrical, with normal dimensions and homogeneous parenchyma in both plain and contrast phases, showing some round, homogeneous hypodense images without enhancement after contrast medium administration, with a diameter of 7 mm, randomly distributed (three in the right kidney and four in the left kidney), with no dilation in the collecting systems or associated with stones. The perirenal fat and hilum are preserved. Bilateral symmetric elimination is observable.\nThe psoas and dorsal-lumbar muscles are within normal limits. The urinary bladder in the pelvic region appears normal, filled with liquid, and with preserved wall characteristics. Pelvic structures appear normal.\n \nDiagnostic Impression:\nThe overall findings suggest diffuse liver disease, to consider hepatic cirrhosis associated with ascites, splenomegaly, and portosystemic periportal, gastroesophageal, and splenorenal formations, as well as recanalization of the falciform ligament and anterior abdominal wall collaterals.\nSecondary edema of the gallbladder wall.\nBosniak I bilateral cysts.\nNo alterations in the rest of the study. A Doppler ultrasound of the liver and bile ducts is suggested for further evaluation.

The ascending, transverse, and descending colon are moderately filled with residual material, with no apparent abnormalities.\nIn the pelvic region, the urinary bladder is normal, filled with fluid, and has a preserved wall.\nThe pelvic structures appear normal.\n \nDiagnostic impression:\nThe findings collectively suggest diffuse liver disease, likely cirrhosis, associated with ascites, splenomegaly, and portosystemic collateral formations in the periportal, gastroesophageal, and splenorenal regions, along with recanalization of the falciform ligament and collateral formations in the anterior abdominal wall.\nGallbladder wall edema secondary to other conditions.\nBilateral Bosniak I renal cysts.\nThe rest of the study shows no significant abnormalities.

CORADS V.\nMediastinal adenopathies with an inflammatory appearance.
A CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis was performed with a multidetector in both the non-contrast phase and after the administration of non-ionic contrast medium via intravenous route, covering from both lung bases to the pubic symphysis. The following findings were observed:\nLung bases show adequate aeration.\nThe liver has a normal shape, size, and position, with homogeneous parenchyma and lobulated margins. The intrahepatic bile ducts have a normal course and caliber.\nThe portal vein measures 16 mm in thickness. The uterus and adnexa are not evaluable.\n \nDiagnostic impression:\nFindings suggestive of hepatic cirrhosis.\nThickening of the stomach wall of undetermined origin.\nSigns of portal hypertension.\nAbundant ascites.\nDiverticulosis in the sigmoid colon.\nThe uterus and adnexa are not evaluable.\nCalcified atheromatosis.\nSpondyloarthrosis.\nThe rest of the study shows no significant abnormalities.
DESCRIPTION: \nFree fluid is identified in the abdominal cavity in the supraphrenic space, Morrison's pouch, paracolic gutters, and splenorenal area. The liver appears small in size with irregular, lobulated borders and a "fine granular" echographic pattern. Partial loss of the interface with muscular structures is observed. The gallbladder is partially distended, with a wall thickness of 5.4 mm and anechoic content.\nColor and spectral Doppler application to the vascular structures show the suprahepatic veins with a loss of their usual pattern, now monophase. The hepatic artery shows a peak systolic velocity of 102.8 cm/second, along with an increase in the resistance index. The portal vein shows increased echogenicity of its content, with absence of flow, but flow is present at the level of the confluence. The common bile duct shows no abnormalities. The pancreas and kidneys show no evident abnormalities. The spleen has a longitudinal axis of 117.2 mm and a splenic volume of 347 cc.\n \nCONCLUSION:\nChronic diffuse liver disease with monophase pattern changes in the spectra of the suprahepatic veins and portal vein thrombosis.\nThe spleen has a larger-than-usual longitudinal axis, but its splenic volume is preserved.\nAscites.

Cardiac MRI  \nDate: 07/12/2024\n\nINDICATION:  \nSuspected Tako Tsubo syndrome.\n\nTECHNIQUE:  \nShort-axis and 4-chamber perfusion sequences were performed, along with short-axis, long-axis, and 4-chamber late enhancement, and short-axis and 4-chamber Cine sequences. DOTAREM 30 ml, lot 23GD079A01.\n\nRESULTS:  \nThe left ventricular ejection fraction is normal, measured at 51%.  \nDue to significant artifacts at the base of the LV, analysis is impossible at this level. No distinct segmental or global wall motion abnormalities are identifiable in the rest of the LV. Specifically, no akinesia is found in the antero-septal-apical or septoapical region today.  \nMyocardial thickness appears preserved.  \nNo left ventricular dilation: LVEDV = 126 ml = 57 ml/m², LVESV = 61 ml.  \nThe right ventricle is not dilated, with good systolic function: RVEF = 51%, RVEDV = 59 ml = 27 ml/m², RVESV = 29 ml.  \nAfter gadolinium injection, no perfusion defect is visualized.  \nOn late enhancement sequences, an intramyocardial contrast uptake is discovered in the infero-lateral-basal region over approximately two segments, appearing rather subendocardial and non-transmural. The presence of a segmental wall motion abnormality at this level cannot be assessed due to artifacts on the cine sequences at this level.  \nNo other intramyocardial contrast uptake is detectable, particularly in the antero-septal-apical or septoapical region.  \nAdditionally, no abnormal intracardiac mass is detectable.  \nNo abnormal pericardial thickening, effusion, or contrast uptake.  \nNo extracardiac abnormalities.\n\nCONCLUSION:  \nThe LV is normokinetic and normodynamic.  \nNo segmental wall motion abnormalities or abnormal contrast uptake are found in the antero-septal-apical or septoapical region.  \nDiscovery of an intramyocardial contrast uptake appearing rather subendocardial in the infero-lateral-basal region over approximately two segments, with segmental wall motion analysis at this level impossible today due to artifacts: myocardial necrosis?  \nTo be correlated with the overall clinical, biological, and imaging findings for follow-up as needed.

This mass is causing a forward pushing effect on the lens.

CT exam acquired before and after i.v. contrast agent injection (100 ml of Iomeron 400) in patient with Budd Chiari syndrome.\nLiver is slightly increased in size, namely with caudate lobe hypertrophy. Liver parenchyma has inhomogeneous enhancement with “nutmeg” appearance and hepatic veins are not visualized. There are innumerable intrahepatic venous shunts with collateral veins draining into superior cava vein. \nMultiple liver nodules, highly hypervascular on arterial phase, and isoattenuating on portal venous and delayed phases, with the largest one being in segment 7 (1 cm), which are suggestive of FNH-like lesions.\nPortal vein and superior mesenteric vein are patent.\nNormal appearance of pancreas, kidney, adrenals, spleen.\nBladder is almost empty, not assessable.\nCystic lesion of 3 cm in the right adnexa, suggestive for dominant ovarian follicle.\nNo enlarged lymph nodes.\nNo free fluid.\nCONCLUSION\nLiver has changes suggestive of Budd Chiari syndrome in a chronic phase. Multiple FNH-like nodules. We suggest to perform next follow-up with MRI with hepatobiliary contrast agent.

Question regarding a malignant lesion and lymph node metastases.The justifying indication for the use of ionizing radiation was provided by the responsible, qualified physician.\nInformation and Consent:After obtaining a risk history, the patient was verbally and in writing informed about the contrast agent application, the examination procedure, and potential risks (also refer to the consent form). Written consent was obtained from the patient.\nExamination Technique:Digital overview radiographs. Intravenous administration of 100 ml Imeron 400 and acquisition of the thorax/abdomen/pelvis in the venous contrast phase. Multiplanar reconstructions. Dose-length product 760.90 mGy*cm.\nFindings:A conventional thoracic image from the same day is available for comparison.\nThorax:The heart is not enlarged. No pericardial effusion. No evidence of a central pulmonary artery embolism, as far as can be assessed in the venous contrast phase. Another enlarged and roundly configured lymph node is found retrocrurally with a size of 9 x 9 mm and inhomogeneous contrast. No pathologically enlarged or configured lymph nodes axillary or left pulmonary hilar. Extensive hypodense space-occupying lesion on the right side centrally, involving the right hilum with a size of 84 x 56 mm, and with immediate contact to the right main bronchus and the paravertebral pleura.
