CORONARY CT ANGIOGRAPHY\n\nIndication: History of hypertension. Reason: Acute coronary syndrome.\n\nProtocol: Biphasic injection of iodinated contrast 370mg/ml. Good quality examination: minor artifacts but remains diagnostically reliable.\n\nResults\n\nCoronary Arteries: No congenital anomalies or abnormal course of the coronary arteries. Right coronary dominance.\n\nLeft System: \nLeft main coronary artery: Originates normally from the left anterior sinus, with an eccentric atheromatous-calcified plaque causing minimal stenosis estimated at 20% and extending over 6.5 mm, showing signs of vulnerability: \n- Punctate calcification (< 3 mm).\n- Positive remodeling (ratio > 1.1 compared to the average diameters upstream and downstream).\n\nLeft anterior descending artery: Infiltrated with multiple atheromatous-calcified stenotic plaques, the most significant being: \n- Ostial, eccentric, causing stenosis estimated at 30% and extending over 5.2 mm.\n- Mid-segment, eccentric, causing stenosis estimated at 63% and extending over 11 mm.\n- Distal segment, circumferential, causing stenosis estimated at 40% and extending over 8.2 mm.\n\nCircumflex artery: Infiltrated with multiple atheromatous-calcified stenotic plaques, the most significant being: \n- Ostial, eccentric, causing stenosis estimated at 43% and extending over 6.4 mm, showing signs of vulnerability (hypodense plaques < 30 HU and positive remodeling).\n- Distal segment, circumferential, causing moderate stenosis estimated at 66% and extending over 17 mm.\n\nRight System: The right coronary artery originates normally from the right anterior sinus. It is infiltrated without focal stenosis.\n\nNo pleuro-parenchymal abnormalities within the limits of the exploration field.\n\nConclusion: CT scan classified as CAD-RADS 3 / P4 / HRP.
RIGHT SHOULDER ARTHROSCAN\n\nIndication\n\nRecurrent instability of the right shoulder.\n\nTechnique\n\nVolumetric acquisition with multiplanar reconstructions following intra-articular injection of a radio-opaque preparation.\n\nResults\n\nNo static glenohumeral abnormalities.\n\nBone and joint analysis:\n- No humeral bone notch.\n- No glenoid bone or cartilage lesions.\n- Intact acromioclavicular and glenohumeral joints.\n\nLabro-ligamentous analysis: No lesions of the glenoid labrum apart from degenerative changes.\n\nTendon analysis:\n- Full-thickness tear of the supraspinatus tendon at its insertion with retraction to the glenoid (Patte type 03), approximately 24 mm from the bicipital groove. Associated fatty degeneration of the muscle belly classified as Goutallier grade 03 with muscle atrophy.\n- Intra-tendinous cleavage of the infraspinatus muscle with fatty degeneration, Goutallier stage 02.\n- Reduced subacromial space estimated at 4 mm.\n- Intact subscapularis tendon.\n- No subluxation of the long head of the biceps tendon.\n- Bursitis of the subacromial-subdeltoid bursa.\n\nConclusion\n\nFull-thickness tear of the supraspinatus tendon associated with intra-tendinous cleavage of the infraspinatus.
CT Angiography of the Supra-Aortic Trunks\n\nIndication:\n\nHistory: Patient is a candidate for bypass surgery.\nReason for request: On Doppler:\n- Severe stenosis of the right subclavian artery with hemodynamic impact.\n- Pseudoaneurysm of the internal carotid artery.\n\nProtocol:\nExamination performed in helical mode after injection of iodinated contrast during the arterial phase.\n\nFindings:\nThe ascending thoracic aorta is of normal caliber, with no wall thickening or detected stenosis.\nNormal arrangement of the supra-aortic trunks.\n\nRight side:\n- Brachiocephalic trunk: patent with no wall abnormalities.\n- Subclavian artery: contains an eccentric, regular, mixed plaque without positive remodeling, no significant stenosis (35%), extending over 18 mm.\n- Common carotid and external carotid arteries: patent with no wall abnormalities.\n- Internal carotid artery: tortuous, with a mixed bulbous plaque containing a moderate number of calcifications, eccentric, causing a subtotal stenosis (> 95%) extending over 25 mm, with opacification of the upstream segment which is collapsed and threadlike.\n- Vertebral artery: contains a plaque in its V0 segment, hypodense, regular, eccentric, without significant stenosis (maximum of 30%), extending over approximately 2 mm.\n\nLeft side:\n- Subclavian artery: patent with no wall abnormalities.\n- Common carotid and external carotid arteries: patent with no wall abnormalities.\n- Internal carotid artery: features:\n  - An eccentric bulbous plaque with a regular surface, mixed with a few minimal calcifications, non-stenosing.\n  - A kinking (angle < 60°) in its post-bulbar segment.\n- Vertebral artery: hypoplastic, contains a plaque in its V1 segment, hypodense, regular, eccentric, without significant stenosis (maximum of 40%), extending over approximately 10 mm.\nThe arteries of the circle of Willis are patent, with no detectable stenosis, except for atherosclerotic infiltration of the basilar trunk.\n\nConclusion:\n\nPre-occlusive stenosis of the left internal carotid artery at its bulbous segment due to an atherosclerotic plaque without vulnerability criteria.
CT SCAN OF THE CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS\n\nIndication\nFRP under treatment, follow-up.\n\nProtocol\nThe examination was performed with volumetric acquisition before and after contrast injection in a tri-phasic mode. No prior radiological documents were available for comparison.\n\nFindings\n\nThere is persistent infiltration of the retroperitoneal fat, presenting as a peri-aortic cuff measuring 31 x 60 mm (W x H), surrounding the sub-renal abdominal aorta over more than 180° with relative sparing of the posterior aspect, extending to the left common iliac artery, with mild enhancement in the delayed phase. \nThere are a few retroperitoneal lymph nodes without concerning features.\nThe aorta is not displaced, remains patent, and shows some parietal calcifications without detectable stenosis.\nIts visceral branches are patent.\nNo stenosis of the inferior vena cava is observed.\nBoth kidneys and the spleen appear normal on CT, with no dilation of the pyelocaliceal systems.\nThe ureters are long and slender, remaining distant from this infiltration.\nThe gonadal veins are preserved.\nNo digestive stenosis is noted.\n\nAdditionally:\nThoracic region:\nNo parenchymal lesions are seen in either the interstitial or alveolar sectors.\nVascular structures are normally positioned, of usual caliber, and patent.\nNo hilar or mediastinal lymphadenopathy is present.\nNo pleural or pericardial effusion is observed.\n\nAbdominal-pelvic region:\nThe liver is of normal size, with homogeneous enhancement and regular contours.\nThe portal vein is patent and of normal caliber.\nNo dilation of the intrahepatic or extrahepatic bile ducts.\nThe gallbladder is not distended, with a thin wall.\nThe pancreas is of normal size, with homogeneous enhancement. No dilation of the ductal structures.\nThe spleen is of normal size and usual density.\nBoth adrenal glands are intact.\nThe bladder has normal capacity, with a thin and regular wall.\nNo intra-peritoneal fluid collection is observed.\nNo suspicious bone lesions are noted in the explored volume.\n\nConclusion:\nAppearance consistent with FRP with persistent mild enhancement. A comparison with the previous examination is recommended to assess treatment progress.
THORACO-ABDOMINO-PELVIC CT SCAN\n\nIndication: Follow-up. Patient history: Metastatic breast cancer under hormone therapy.\n\nProtocol:\nVolumetric slices with fine multi-planar reconstruction, both with and without contrast injection. Follow-up examination compared to the one from 02/20/2024.\n\nResults:\n\nThoracic region:\nIncrease in size of the right breast nodule in the deep inner quadrant, measuring 27 x 22 x 21 mm (AP x T x H) compared to 18 x 16 x 16 mm.\nEnlargement of axillary lymph nodes in Berg zones I and III, roughly rounded with irregular outer margins, enhanced, one with central necrosis.\nNo axillary, supraclavicular, or internal mammary lymphadenopathy on the left side.\nHilar and mediastinal lymph node chains are preserved.\nNo suspicious pulmonary nodule.\nNo pleural effusion or significant pleural thickening.\nNo pericardial effusion.\nTracheobronchial axis is clear.\n\nAbdomino-pelvic region:\nLiver is enlarged (FH at 150 mm), generally hypodense (< 40 HU with a hepato-splenic gradient > 10 HU), with well-defined regular contours, no obvious signs of dysmorphia. It contains a nodular lesion straddling segments V and VI, subcapsular, with peripheral nodular enhancement and progressive filling, stable since the exam on 06/11/2020, approximately 30 mm in long axis, consistent with a typical hepatic hemangioma.\nSplenic nodular formation, rounded with well-defined regular contours, measuring 20 x 16 x 18 mm (T x AP x H), slightly hypodense compared to splenic parenchyma, mildly enhanced post-contrast, consistent with a secondary localization.\nRight adrenal nodular formation, rounded and well-defined, 10 mm in long axis (compared to 07 mm on the exam from 06/11/2020).\nLeft adrenal gland is preserved.\nNo significant intra or retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy.\nNo peritoneal effusion, particularly in the Douglas pouch.\n\nAdditionally:\nPortal vein is patent, with normal caliber.\nNo dilation of intra or extrahepatic bile ducts.\nGallbladder is not distended, with a thin wall.\nPancreas is of normal size, with homogeneous enhancement. No ductal structure dilation.\nSpleen is of normal size and usual density.\nAdrenals show no abnormalities.\nKidneys are of normal size and position, opacified within usual timeframes without pyelocaliceal cavity dilation.\nSimple renal cyst, upper left pole, with internal exophytic development measuring 15 x 22 mm in long axes, classified as Bosniak I.\nBladder has normal capacity, with a thin and regular wall.\nDefect in the linea alba above the umbilicus measuring 34 x 30 mm (T x H), allowing passage of a hernial sac with epiploic content measuring 47 x 79 x 94 mm (T x AP x H) without signs of complications.\n\nBone structure:\nPersistence of the sacrococcygeal lytic bone lesion, extending to the 2nd, 3rd, and 4th sacral vertebrae as well as the coccyx, involving the corresponding sacral foramina and extending to the posterior arches, heterogeneous due to the presence of some bony debris within, with poorly defined fuzzy contours and cortical disruption in places.\nOsteoblastic lesion of the left greater trochanter, stable compared to the exam from 05/24/2023.\nNo other suspicious bone lesion in the explored volume.\n\nConclusion:\n\nRESICT: Disease progression.\nSignificant increase in the secondary-appearing splenic lesion.\nIncrease in the volume of the right breast mass.
BRAIN MRI\n\nIndication\n\nHistory: Down syndrome.\nReason: Investigation of recurrent ischemic strokes.\n\nProtocol\n\nAxial slices in FLAIR, diffusion, T2 GRE, sagittal in T1, and coronal in T2.\n3D TOF and 3D T1 vascular sequences post-gadolinium injection.\n\nFindings\n\nMultiple bilateral lacunar lesions in the semi-oval centers, with signal characteristics similar to CSF on all sequences, of ischemic origin localized to the deep watershed territory.\nLeft parietal porencephalic cavity, surrounded by peripheral gliosis, related to a chronic stroke in the posterior superficial watershed territory.\nNo recent ischemic lesions on the diffusion sequence.\nA left lenticular microbleed is observed.\nThe vascular sequence shows stenosis of the terminal portions of the carotid arteries as well as the initial segments of the right MCA at its M1 portion, the entire M1 portion of the left MCA, and the A1 portions of the ACAs bilaterally. Flow is maintained through multiple collaterals at the level of the basal ganglia, giving a "puff of smoke" appearance, resulting in flow voids on the T2/FLAIR sequence. No development of collateral circulation from the posterior circulation or the external carotid artery is noted.\nThe superficial temporal arteries are patent with satisfactory caliber.\nPerfusion sequences show a decrease in rCBF in the frontal regions bilaterally, contrasting with normal to increased rCBV and prolonged MTT in some areas. Perfusion aspects in the posterior circulation territories are preserved.\n\nConclusion\n\nMRI features consistent with Moyamoya syndrome with predominant frontal hypoperfusion, more pronounced on the left.
BRAIN MRI\n\nIndication\nHistory: Hypertension.\nReason: Sudden onset of altered consciousness.\n\nProtocol\nAxial slices in FLAIR, diffusion, SWI, sagittal in T1, and coronal in T2.\n3D TOF vascular sequences and 3D T1 post-gadolinium injection.\n\nFindings\nRight fronto-parietal hematoma extending to the Sylvian fissure, roughly oval-shaped, well-defined, measuring 47 x 53 x 31 mm (AP x T x H, with a volume of 40 cc), appearing as T2 hypointense with significant blooming on SWI, hypo to isointense on T1, indicating its acute nature, surrounded by an edematous area with T2/FLAIR hyperintensity, diffusion hyperintensity with marked restriction related to an ischemic component.\nIt is expansive, causing subfalcine herniation with a midline shift measured at 6 mm.\nBilateral filling of the convexity sulci as well as the basal cisterns and right Sylvian fissure, appearing as T2/FLAIR hyperintense, SWI hypointense.\nMinimal intraventricular hemorrhage in both occipital horns as well as the third and fourth ventricles, appearing as T2/FLAIR hyperintense, SWI hypointense, without ventricular system dilation.\n\nIdentification of a saccular aneurysm at the M1/M2 junction of the right MCA with the following characteristics:\n• Measurements: 2 mm (neck) x 4 mm (length).\n• Dome/neck ratio: 2.\n• Margins: regular.\n• Direction: downward, inward, and forward.\n• Opacification: remains patent.\nDural venous sinuses are patent.\n\nAdditionally:\nRounded signal abnormalities in the deep periventricular white matter, non-expansive, appearing as T2/FLAIR hyperintense without diffusion correlation, not enhanced post-contrast.\nPosterior fossa: no signal abnormality in the brainstem or cerebellar hemispheres.\nNo abnormality at the cervico-occipital junction.\n\nConclusion\nRuptured saccular aneurysm at the M1/M2 junction of the right MCA complicated by an acute intraparenchymal hematoma associated with a Fisher grade IV subarachnoid hemorrhage.\nParenchymal ischemia in the superficial territory of the right MCA.
BRAIN MRI\n\nIndication\nHistory: Heart disease under treatment.\nReason: Consciousness disturbances with hemiplegia and left facial palsy.\n\nProtocol\nAxial slices in FLAIR, diffusion, T2 GRE, sagittal in T1, and coronal in T2.\n3D TOF vascular sequences and 3D T1 post-gadolinium injection.\n\nFindings\nA lesion area in the right fronto-parieto-temporal cortico-subcortical region, corresponding to the territory of the ipsilateral MCA and ACA, appears as T2/FLAIR hyperintense, T1 hypointense, diffusion hyperintense (b1000) with restricted diffusion, heterogeneous due to linear/punctate hemorrhagic changes and a hematoma-like T2* hypointense area, occupying less than 30% of the infarct volume (38 x 20 x 32 mm AP x T x H, with a volume of 126 cc), classified as PH1 according to the ECASS 02 classification. There is also a minimal intraventricular hemorrhage in the occipital horn of the ipsilateral lateral ventricle.\n\nThis area exerts a mass effect on the adjacent brain parenchyma, with:\n• Subfalcine herniation with a midline shift estimated at 18 mm.\n• Right temporal herniation with paradoxical widening of the quadrigeminal cistern.\n• Left exclusion hydrocephalus without signs of transependymal resorption.\n\nOn the vascular sequence, there is an occlusion of the internal carotid artery starting from its C5 segment with downstream flow slowing signs.\n\nAdditionally:\nRounded signal abnormalities in the deep and periventricular white matter above the tentorium, non-expansive, appearing as T2/FLAIR hyperintense without diffusion restriction, not enhanced post-contrast, classified as FAZEKAS 02. Brainstem and cerebellar hemispheres show no signal abnormalities.\nDural venous sinuses are patent.\nNo abnormalities at the cervico-occipital junction.\n\nConclusion\nAcute ischemic stroke in the territories of the right MCA and ACA due to occlusion of the C5 segment of the internal carotid artery, with PH1 type hemorrhagic changes, causing subfalcine and right temporal herniations.
BRAIN MRI\n\nIndication\n\nHistory: Meningioma operated on in 2017.  \nReason: Increased headaches with left exophthalmos.\n\nProtocol\n\nAxial slices in FLAIR, diffusion, T2 GRE, sagittal in T1, and coronal in T2.  \n3D T1 sequences after gadolinium injection.\n\nResults\n\nExam compared to that of 12/28/2023:  \nStability of the extra-axial tumor process in the middle and posterior skull base, left-sided, with well-defined regular borders, currently measuring 48 x 46 x 37 mm (T x H x AP) (compared to 45 x 48 x 34 mm on the 12/28/2023 exam), appearing as T2 hyperintense, T1 isointense, and strongly enhanced after gadolinium injection.  \nIt presents the following extensions:  \n- Medially: to the sellar region, displacing the pituitary parenchyma upwards and laterally, and to both cavernous sinuses, encasing the internal carotid arteries 360° without reducing their caliber.  \n- Laterally: reaching the left temporal lobe.  \n- Anteriorly: to Meckel's cave and intra-orbitally via the superior orbital fissure, surrounding the optic nerve, which remains of normal signal. This extension is responsible for grade 02 exophthalmos (orbit-bi-canthal line distance of 03 mm).  \n- Posteriorly: filling the pre-pontine and left ambient cisterns, exerting a mass effect on the pons and left cerebral peduncle without signal abnormality, and displacing the basilar artery to the right without caliber abnormality.  \n- Superiorly: filling the suprasellar cistern and reaching the optic chiasm, without signal abnormality.  \nCerebellar tonsillar descent 10 mm below the foramen magnum.  \nVentricular system of normal size.  \nSequelae changes in the left temporal parenchyma with passive dilation of the left lateral ventricle's temporal horn.  \nSome signal abnormalities in the deep supratentorial white matter, appearing as T2/FLAIR hyperintense without diffusion sequence translation, no enhancement after gadolinium injection, nonspecific.  \nNote: Fluid filling of the left mastoid cells.\n\nConclusion\n\nRelative stability of the meningioma compared to the exam of 12/28/2023.

This is causing an enlargement of the sella with bulging of the diaphragm and sellar floor.\nIt presents the following extensions:\n Supra-sellar: Partial filling of the supra-sellar cistern without reaching the optic chiasm, which is of normal morphology and signal.\n Infra-sellar: Well-aerated sphenoidal sinus.\n Lateral-sellar:\n Extension into the right cavernous sinus classified as Knosp 01.\n Left cavernous sinus remains unaffected.\nPituitary parenchyma displaced to the left, normal size (4 mm in height on coronal slice), with homogeneous signal.\nPosterior pituitary shows physiological spontaneous T1 hyperintensity, in place.\nPituitary stalk of normal thickness, deviated to the left.\n\nConclusion:\n\nStability of the pituitary macroadenoma compared to the exam of 01/25/2024.

Hypothalamic-Pituitary MRI Report\n\nIndication:\n\nHistory: Not specified.  \nReason for consultation: Hyperprolactinemia 80 ng/mL.\n\nProtocol:\n\nSagittal and coronal TSE T1-weighted images without and with gadolinium contrast.  \nCoronal TSE T2-weighted images without gadolinium contrast.\n\nResults:\n\nNo detectable intra-sellar nodular lesion or indirect signs suggestive of such, including:  \n- No deviation of the pituitary stalk.  \n- No convexity of the sellar diaphragm.  \n- No lowering of the sellar floor.  \n- No asymmetry or lateralization of the anterior pituitary.  \nThe pituitary parenchyma is of normal size and homogeneous signal.  \nThe posterior pituitary shows physiological spontaneous T1 hyperintensity and is in place.  \nThe pituitary stalk is of normal thickness.  \nThe suprasellar cistern is clear.  \nThe optic chiasm has normal morphology and usual signal.  \nThe third ventricle is of normal size and in place.  \nThe cavernous sinuses are clear.  \nThe sphenoid sinus is well-aerated.\n\nConclusion:\n\nNo detectable hypothalamic-pituitary lesion on this examination.
Cervical Spine MRI\n\nIndication:\n\nHistory: Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes.\nReason for consultation: Bilateral C6 cervicobrachial pain.\n\nProtocol:\nSagittal TSE T1-weighted images without fat saturation.\n3D space T2-weighted images without fat saturation. Examination performed without gadolinium injection.\n\nFindings:\n\nNo obvious spinal dysostosis at the occipito-vertebral junction or lower cervical spine.\nReversal of cervical lordosis noted.\nNo vertebral compression.\nNo suspicious signal abnormalities in the vertebral spongy bone.\nSome degenerative changes in the spine without zygapophyseal joint osteoarthritis.\nNo canal narrowing or foraminal stenosis.\nSpinal cord examined up to T7: homogeneous signal.\nNo signal abnormalities or masses in the posterior cervical fossa within the explored volume.\nPerivertebral soft tissues are preserved.\n\nConclusion:\nNo disc-root conflicts, particularly at the C6 level.
MRI OF THE LUMBAR SPINE\n\nIndication:\nHistory: Not specified. Reason for consultation: Low back pain with sciatica/back pain.\n\nProtocol:\nSagittal TSE T1-weighted images without fat saturation. Sagittal and axial TSE T2-weighted images without fat saturation. Examination performed without gadolinium injection.\n\nResults:\n\nNo obvious spinal dysostosis in the examined segment.\nPostural abnormality in the form of scoliosis with the following characteristics:\n• Cobb angle estimated at 14.25°.\n• Left convexity with the apex at L1.\n• Limiting vertebrae T11 - L4.\nMild anterolisthesis (< 25%) of L3 over L4 due to subluxation of the facet joints.\nMild vertebral compression of L2 and L3 appearing benign.\nNo suspicious signal abnormalities in the bone.\nDegenerative changes in the spine, notably with multilevel facet joint osteoarthritis.\nAt the L1-L2 level: circumferential disc bulge with posterior osteophyte formation without canal narrowing or disc-root conflict.\nAt the L2-L3 level: circumferential disc bulge with hypertrophy of the ligamentum flavum causing:\n• Canal narrowing classified as grade B according to Schizas (AP diameter 15 mm).\n• Bilateral lateral recess stenosis, more pronounced on the right.\n• No foraminal stenosis.\nAt the L3-L4 level: circumferential disc bulge with hypertrophy of the ligamentum flavum causing:\n• Canal narrowing classified as grade C according to Schizas (AP diameter 09 mm).\n• Bilateral lateral recess stenosis at L4.\n• Mild left foraminal stenosis.\nAt the L4-L5 and L5-S1 levels: disc bulges without canal narrowing or disc-root conflict.\nConus medullaris projects at L1.\nNo signal abnormalities in the spinal cord or nerve roots in the examined segments.\nPreservation of the perivertebral soft tissues.\n\nConclusion:\n\nDegenerative spine with multilevel canal narrowing, more severe at the L3-L4 level, associated with lateral recess stenosis.
ABDOMINO-PELVIC ULTRASOUND\n\nIndication\n\nPatient history: No significant medical history.\nReason: Right lower quadrant pain with abnormal blood work (12,000 WBC).\n\nProtocol\n\nThe examination was performed on an emergency basis using a 5 MHz convex probe and a 9 MHz superficial probe.\n\nFindings\n\nThe appendix, located internally and laterally to the cecum, was fully visualized and found to be enlarged, measuring 11 mm in diameter, while maintaining its usual layering. No fecalith was observed. No sonographically detectable fluid collection was visualized. There is fat stranding in the right lower quadrant with mesenteric hyperemia. A few lymph nodes in the right lower quadrant appear reactive. No intraperitoneal fluid accumulation was noted.\n\nAdditionally:\nThe liver is of normal size, with a homogeneous echotexture and usual echogenicity, and has regular contours. There is no dilation of the intrahepatic or extrahepatic bile ducts. The gallbladder is free of stones and has a thin wall. The pancreas, visualized in its body and head portions, is of normal size and echotexture. The spleen is of normal size and homogeneous echotexture. Both kidneys are of normal size and position, maintaining good corticomedullary differentiation. There is no dilation of the renal collecting systems. The bladder is partially filled, with anechoic content.\n\nConclusion\n\nUltrasound findings are suggestive of acute appendicitis without signs of complication at this time.
THYROID ULTRASOUND\n\nIndication\n\nPatient history: No significant findings.\nReason: Palpable cervical nodular lesion.\n\nProtocol\n\nThe examination was performed using a high-frequency 12MHz probe.\n\nResults\n\nThe thyroid is of normal size with the following measurements:\n Right lobe: estimated volume of 06 cm³.\n Left lobe: estimated volume of 07 cm³.\n Isthmus is thin and regular.\n No mediastinal extension.\nThe thyroid exhibits normal echogenicity and homogeneous echotexture, with usual vascularization on color Doppler.\nTwo thyroid nodules with similar characteristics were identified: solid-cystic, oval-shaped with harmonious axes, well-defined regular borders, with the solid portion being isoechoic compared to the adjacent thyroid parenchyma, and no microcalcifications observed within, classifying them as EU-TIRADS 03. Their locations and measurements are as follows:\n N1: upper right lobe, measuring 07 x 12 x 09 mm in major axes (AP x T x H).\n N2: middle left lobe, measuring 04 x 07 x 06 mm in major axes (AP x T x H).\nMultiple bilateral cervical lymph node formations without adverse echographic criteria.\n\nConclusion\n\nTwo thyroid nodules classified as EU-TIRADS 03. A follow-up in 1-2 years is recommended.
SCROTAL ULTRASOUND\n\nIndication\n\nPatient history: Type 2 diabetes.\nReason: Painful, swollen, warm scrotum.\n\nProtocol\n\nExam performed with a high-frequency 12MHz probe.\n\nFindings\n\nRight testicle:\n Enlarged (measuring 33 cc), generally hypoechoic, with increased blood flow on color Doppler.\n Increased blood flow on color Doppler with decreased resistance indices on pulsed Doppler (0.3).\n Epididymis swollen in all three parts, generally hypoechoic.\n Spermatic cord swollen, infiltrated, generally hyperechoic, with no visible twisting.\n Moderate hydrocele.\n Diffuse thickening of the scrotal layers observed.\n No detectable collection.\n\nLeft testicle:\n Normal size and echostructure, with normal color Doppler uptake.\n Epididymis of normal size and echostructure.\n No twisting observed.\n No hydrocele.\n\nConclusion\n\nUltrasound findings suggest right-sided orchiepididymitis without signs of complications at this time.
ABDOMINO-PELVIC ULTRASOUND\n\nIndication\n\nPatient history: Type 2 diabetes, ischemic heart disease. \nReason: Diffuse abdominal pain.\n\nProtocol\n\nExamination performed with a 5 MHz convex probe.\n\nResults\n\nThe liver is enlarged (CRL at 170 mm) with a homogeneous echotexture, regular contours, and is hyperechoic with a high hepato-renal gradient. No sonographically detectable hepatic nodular lesions. No dilation of the intrahepatic and extrahepatic bile ducts. Gallbladder is free of stones, with a thin wall. The pancreas is visualized in its body and head portions, with normal size and echotexture. The spleen is of normal size and homogeneous echotexture. Both kidneys are of normal size and position, maintaining good cortico-medullary differentiation. No dilation of the pyelocaliceal cavities. The bladder is partially filled, with an anechoic content. No sonographically detectable thickening of the digestive wall. No intra-peritoneal fluid collection.\n\nConclusion\n\nUltrasound findings are consistent with a liver overload, likely due to homogeneous and diffuse hepatic steatosis.
THYROID ULTRASOUND\n\nIndication\n\nPatient history: Crohn's disease.\nReason: Abnormal thyroid function tests.\n\nProtocol\n\nExam performed with a high-frequency 12MHz probe.\n\nResults\n\nThe thyroid gland is enlarged with the following measurements:\n	Right lobe: estimated volume of 12 cm³.\n	Left lobe: estimated volume of 10 cm³.\n	Thickened isthmus: estimated volume of 2 cm³.\nThe thyroid is globally hypoechoic and heterogeneous due to the presence of hyperechoic septa, giving a pseudo-nodular appearance.\nColor Doppler shows hyperemia of the thyroid parenchyma.\nPulsed Doppler reveals increased velocities in all four thyroid arteries, averaging 60 cm/s.\nNo thyroid nodules are present.\nMultiple bilateral cervical lymph nodes are noted without any concerning ultrasound features.\n\nConclusion\n\nUltrasound findings are consistent with a goiter due to thyroiditis, to be correlated with TSH levels.

The cardiac silhouette is of normal size, with an accentuated waist and prominent outlines of the right middle part of the mediastinal silhouette (valve defect? only an effect of positional asymmetry? - verification with echocardiography and large vessels).

Description of the study: The CT of the head was performed without contrast enhancement. No signs of acute intracranial bleeding were identified. Ambiguous appearance of the left temporal lobe - a small hyperdense area of up to about 15mm is visible - possibly an artifact from bone, indicating assessment in an MRI study. Otherwise, the density of the white and gray matter is normal. The supratentorial ventricular system is of normal size, not displaced. The posterior fossa structures and fourth ventricle show no changes. No changes were observed in the bony structures of the skull.

Data: A 29-year-old patient with suspected ischemic stroke of the left cerebral hemisphere, hypertensive crisis, ACoA aneurysm, and uncontrolled hypertension. Artifacts are seen around the Yasargil clip.\nIn the left frontal region, near the frontal horn of the left lateral ventricle, a 3.0 cm x 2.2 cm x 2.9 cm fluid-filled area is visible, suggestive of a porencephalic cyst. Similar small cavities are also seen in the cortex and subcortically in the left frontal lobe. In the surrounding area, a zone of increased signal in T2-weighted images is visible, affecting the cortex of the frontal lobe over a short segment. A hyperintense T2 and hypointense T1 area involves the cortex and white matter of the temporal lobe. A much smaller area of similar changes is seen parasagittally in the left occipital lobe. Secondary dilation of the left lateral ventricle is observed.\nThe findings suggest extensive past ischemic changes in the left frontal, temporal, and parietal lobes. Otherwise, the brain and intracranial fluid spaces are normal. No midline shift is noted.\nAfter contrast administration, no pathological intracranial enhancement was detected. Arteries, veins, and venous sinuses enhanced normally. In the SWI sequence, two signal voids in the left frontal lobe suggest hemosiderin accumulation. No areas of diffusion restriction were detected intracranially.\nConclusion: Past ischemic changes with porencephalic cyst formation in the areas of previous infarction. Dilation of the left lateral ventricle secondary to hemispheric volume loss is observed.

Hysterosalpingography\n\nIndication:\nEvaluation of secondary infertility.\n\nTechnique:\nPlain film centered on the pelvis.\nOpacification of the uterine cavity using a water-soluble contrast agent.\nImages taken during filling in the frontal view, followed by post-evacuation images.\n\nResults:\nNo calcified opacity on the plain film.\nDiffuse subendometrial microcystic formations consistent with adenomyosis.\nBilateral and synchronous opacification of the fallopian tubes, appearing normal.\nBilateral peritoneal dispersion of the contrast agent, without compartmentalization.\nNo abnormalities in the cervical uterine region on the evacuation image.\n\nConclusion:\nBoth fallopian tubes are patent, with free dispersion of the contrast agent intraperitoneally.\nDiffuse subendometrial microcystic formations consistent with diffuse internal adenomyosis.
Renal MRI Report\n\nIndication:\nPost-treatment follow-up of urothelial carcinoma.\n\nProtocol:\nAxial and coronal T2 sequences, diffusion, T1 Dixon before and after contrast injection with excretory phase.\n\nResults:\nNo previous examination available for comparison.\n\nBladder is partially filled, with no wall abnormalities.\nNo suspicious nodular image or contrast enhancement in the urinary tract.\nNo pelvic or retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy.\nA purely cystic, unilocular formation measuring 29 x 21 mm in the left external iliac region, non-compressive, consistent with a lymphocele, to be correlated with the surgical procedure performed (lymph node dissection?).\n\nKidneys are of normal size and morphology.\nNo dilation of the pyelocaliceal cavities.\nNodular lesion in the upper pole of the right kidney measuring 9 x 8 mm, T2 hypointense with diffusion restriction and late contrast enhancement, initially suggestive of a papillary carcinoma.\n\nAdrenals, liver, pancreas, and spleen are morphologically intact.\nNo focal lesions in the skeletal framework.\n\nConclusion:\nNo evidence to suggest recurrence of urothelial neoplasia.\nCentimeter-sized nodule in the upper pole of the right kidney consistent with a papillary carcinoma.
Prostate MRI\n\nIndication:\nElevated PSA levels. No suspicious lesion on digital rectal exam.\n\nProtocol:\nMultiparametric PI-RADS analysis (T2, diffusion, and perfusion).\n\nFindings:\n\nProstatic hypertrophy measuring 68 x 52 x 57 mm, approximately 100 cc.\nGrade III intravesical protrusion of the median lobe (measuring 14 mm).\nSignificant thickening of the detrusor muscle, without diverticula.\n\nPeripheral zone compressed by the adenoma, with scattered T2 hyperintensity changes, no focal diffusion restriction.\nHypertrophied transition zone, with some changes not affecting interpretation, no significant lesion.\nThin anterior fibromuscular stroma, very hyperintense on T2,\nDiffusion, no significant lesion.\n\nSeminal vesicles appear normal.\nNo pelvic lymphadenopathy.\nNo suspicious bone lesions.\n\nConclusion:\nNo suspicious prostatic lesion.\nProstatic hypertrophy estimated at 100 cc associated with detrusor thickening.
Liver MRI\n\nIndication:\nScreening for hepatocellular carcinoma in the context of viral cirrhosis with elevated alpha-fetoprotein.\n\nProtocol:\nAxial T2, diffusion, and T1 sequences. Post-contrast dynamic T1 FS and delayed DIXON acquisition.\n\nFindings:\n\nLiver dysmorphia with hypertrophy of the left lobe and moderately nodular contours.\nMildly heterogeneous delayed enhancement of the liver parenchyma indicative of fibrosis.\nNo hypervascular focal lesion suggestive of hepatocarcinoma.\nNo biliary duct dilation. Gallbladder without stones.\nPatency of the afferent and efferent vessels.\n\nHomogeneous splenomegaly (measuring 15 cm in its longest axis).\nNo porto-systemic venous shunts.\nNo peritoneal effusion.\n\nNo lymphadenopathy.\nIntact adrenal glands, kidneys, and pancreas.\nNo suspicious bone lesions.\n\nConclusion:\nLiver dysmorphia without suspicious focal lesions.\nSplenomegaly suggestive of portal hypertension.
CORONARY CT ANGIOGRAPHY AND CALCIUM SCORE\n\n73-year-old male patient with chest pain.\n\nCOMPARISON: None.\n\nTECHNIQUE: A preliminary study was conducted using a Canon Aquilion One scanner, followed by the coronary artery calcium protocol. Post intravenous contrast administration, images with 0.5 mm collimation were obtained along the coronary arteries. Data was transferred offline for 3D reconstructions.\n\nACQUISITION: Prospective ECG triggering was used. Heart rate during imaging was approximately 83 bpm.\n\nMEDICATIONS: 100 mg oral metoprolol was administered prior to the scan. 0.4 mg sublingual nitroglycerin was given immediately before the scan.\n\nTECHNICAL QUALITY: Excellent, no artifacts.\n\nFINDINGS:  \n- The calcium score is 686, placing it in the 93rd percentile for age and gender.\n- Coronary arteries are in normal position. Co-dominance of coronary arteries is present.\n\nLeft Main: The left main coronary artery is a short vessel bifurcating into the LAD and LCX. No plaque or stenosis is present.\n\nLAD: Calcified and non-calcified plaques with vessel wall irregularities causing up to 70% stenosis are noted in the proximal and mid LAD, more pronounced proximally. A 6 mm long Type 2 myocardial bridge is observed in the distal LAD. A non-calcified plaque causing 70% stenosis is identified at the ostial level of the D1 branch.\n\nLCX: Calcified and non-calcified plaques with vessel wall irregularities causing up to 70% stenosis are present in the mid LCX segment. Obtuse marginal branches are patent.\n\nRCA: Calcified and non-calcified plaques with vessel wall irregularities causing 60-70% stenosis are noted in the proximal-mid RCA segments. The posterior descending artery and posterior left ventricular branch are patent.\n\nNon-coronary cardiac findings:  \n- Heart valves: The aortic valve is tri-leaflet. There is no thickening or calcification of the aortic and mitral valves.\n- Pericardium: The pericardial contour is preserved, with no effusion, thickening, or calcification.\n- Extracardiac findings: No significant extracardiac findings in the limited views of the lungs, mediastinum, and upper abdomen.\n\nIMPRESSION:  \n1. Stenosis: Significant (>70%) stenosis in the proximal-mid LAD.  \n2. Myocardial bridge: 6 mm long Type 2 myocardial bridge in the distal LAD.  \n3. Stenosis: Significant (60-70%) stenosis in the proximal-mid RCA.  \n4. Plaque: Overall, the calcium score is 686, placing it in the 93rd percentile for age and gender.\n\nRECOMMENDATION: \nCAD RADS 4: P4  \n- Consideration of invasive coronary angiography (ICA) or functional assessment.  \n- Aggressive modification of risk factors and preventive pharmacotherapy is recommended.  \n- Anti-anginal therapy and revascularization options should be considered in line with guidelines. \n\nOther:
CORONARY CT ANGIOGRAPHY AND CALCIUM SCORE\n\n54-year-old male patient with chest pain.\n\nCOMPARISON: None.\n\nTECHNIQUE: A preliminary study was conducted using a Canon Aquilion One scanner, followed by the coronary artery calcium protocol. Post intravenous contrast administration, images with 0.5 mm collimation were obtained along the coronary arteries. Data were transferred offline for 3D reconstructions.\n\nACQUISITION: Prospective ECG gating was used. Heart rate during imaging was approximately 94 bpm.\n\nMEDICATIONS: 100 mg of oral metoprolol was administered prior to the scan. 0.4 mg of sublingual nitroglycerin was given immediately before the scan.\n\nTECHNICAL QUALITY: Acceptable, with moderate artifacts present.\n\nFINDINGS:  \n- The coronary artery calcium score is 111, placing it in the moderate risk category according to the Agatston method. It is in the 97th percentile for age and ethnicity according to MESA.\n- Coronary arteries are in normal position. Right coronary artery dominance is present.\n\nLeft Main: The left main coronary artery is a short vessel bifurcating into the LAD and LCX. No plaque or stenosis is present.\n\nLAD: Millimetric calcified plaques causing 25-30% stenosis are identified in the proximal and mid LAD segments. A calcified plaque causing 45% stenosis is present in the mid segment of the D1 branch.\n\nLCX: No plaque or stenosis is present. Obtuse marginal branches are patent.\n\nRCA: A non-calcified millimetric plaque causing 45% stenosis is identified in the proximal RCA. The posterior descending artery and posterior left ventricular branch are patent.\n\nNon-coronary cardiac findings:\n- Heart valves: The aortic valve is tri-leaflet. There is no thickening or calcification of the aortic and mitral valves.\n- Pericardium: The pericardial contour is preserved, with no effusion, thickening, or calcification.\n- Extra-cardiac findings: No significant extra-cardiac findings in the limited views of the lungs, mediastinum, and upper abdomen.\n\nIMPRESSION:\n1. Stenosis: Mild (25-30%) stenosis in the proximal-mid LAD.  \n2. Stenosis: Mild (45%) stenosis in the mid D1.  \n3. Stenosis: Mild (45%) stenosis in the proximal RCA.  \n4. Plaque: Overall, the coronary artery calcium score is 111, placing it in the moderate risk category according to the Agatston method. It is in the 97th percentile for age and ethnicity according to MESA.\n5. Modifiers: None.\n\nRECOMMENDATION:  \nCAD RADS 2: P2  \n- Non-atherosclerotic causes should be considered.  \n- Risk factor modification and preventive pharmacotherapy are recommended.\n\nOther:

No plaque or stenosis observed.\n\nLAD: Sequential mixed, calcified, and non-calcified plaques with wall irregularities are present in the LAD, with 70-85% stenosis in the proximal-mid segment. Diagonal branches are patent.\n\nLCX: Calcified and non-calcified plaques are present in the LCX, with 30-50% stenosis in the mid segment. Obtuse marginal branches are patent.\n\nRCA: Non-calcified plaques and wall irregularities are present in the RCA, with 40-60% stenosis in the mid segment. Stenosis: Significant (70-85%) stenosis in the proximal-mid LAD.\n2. Stenosis: Mild (30-50%) stenosis in the proximal LCX.\n3. Stenosis: Mild (30-50%) stenosis in the proximal RCA.\n4. Myocardial Bridge: Approximately 3 cm long Type 2 myocardial bridge observed in the distal LAD.\n5. Plaque: Overall, the calcium score is 201, in the 88th percentile for age and gender.\n\nRECOMMENDATION:  \nCAD RADS 0/E  \n- Assurance. Consider non-atherosclerotic causes for symptoms.

CORONARY CT ANGIOGRAPHY AND CALCIUM SCORE\n\n59-year-old male patient with chest pain.\n\nCOMPARISON: None.\n\nTECHNIQUE: A preliminary study was conducted using a Canon Aquilion One scanner, followed by the coronary artery calcium protocol. Post intravenous contrast administration, images with 0.5 mm collimation were obtained along the coronary arteries. Data was transferred offline for 3D reconstructions.\n\nACQUISITION: Prospective ECG gating was used. The heart rate during imaging was approximately 94 bpm.\n\nMEDICATIONS: 100 mg of oral metoprolol was administered prior to the scan. 0.4 mg of sublingual nitroglycerin was given immediately before the scan.\n\nTECHNICAL QUALITY: Acceptable, with moderate artifacts present.\n\nFINDINGS:\n\n- The coronary artery calcium score is 27, placing it in the low-risk category according to the Agatston method. This score is in the 80th percentile for age and gender.\n- Coronary arteries are in a normal position, with right coronary artery dominance.\n\nLeft Main: The left main coronary artery is a short vessel bifurcating into the LAD and LCX. No plaque or stenosis is present.\n\nLAD: The left anterior descending artery (LAD) shows diffuse mixed calcified and non-calcified plaques and vessel wall irregularities in the proximal, mid, and distal segments. There is up to 30-40% stenosis in this area. A 10 mm Type 1 myocardial bridge is observed in the proximal segment of the LAD. Diagonal branches are patent.\n\nRamus Intermedius: No significant stenosis is observed.\n\nLCX: No plaque or stenosis is observed in the left circumflex artery. Obtuse marginal branches are patent.\n\nRCA: Less than 25% stenosis is observed in the proximal segment of the right coronary artery, with non-calcified plaques and vessel wall irregularities. The posterior descending artery and posterior left ventricular branch are patent.\n\nNon-Coronary Cardiac Findings:\n\nHeart Valves: The aortic valve is trileaflet, with no thickening or calcification in the aortic and mitral valves.\n\nPericardium: The pericardial contour is preserved. No effusion, thickening, or calcification is present.\n\nExtracardiac Findings: Limited views of the lungs, mediastinum, and upper abdomen show no significant extracardiac findings.\n\nIMPRESSION:\n1. Stenosis: Mild (25-49%) stenosis in the proximal, mid, and distal segments of the LAD.\n2. Myocardial Bridge: A 10 mm Type 1 myocardial bridge is observed in the proximal segment of the LAD.\n3. Stenosis: Mild (25-49%) stenosis in the proximal segment of the RCA.\n4. Plaque: Overall, the coronary artery calcium score is 27, indicating a low-risk category.\n5. Modifiers: None.\n\nRECOMMENDATION:\nCAD RADS 2: P1\n- Consideration should be given to non-atherosclerotic causes of symptoms.\n- Risk factor modification and preventive pharmacotherapy should be considered.
CORONARY CT ANGIOGRAPHY AND CALCIUM SCORE\n\n48-year-old male patient with complaints of chest pain.\n\nCOMPARISON: None.\n\nTECHNIQUE: A preliminary study was conducted using a Canon Aquilion One scanner, followed by the coronary artery calcium protocol. Post intravenous contrast administration, images with 0.5 mm collimation were obtained along the coronary arteries. Data was transferred offline for 3D reconstructions.\n\nACQUISITION: Prospective ECG gating was used. The heart rate during imaging was approximately 66 bpm.\n\nMEDICATIONS: 100 mg of oral metoprolol was administered prior to the scan. 0.4 mg of sublingual nitroglycerin was given immediately before the scan.\n\nTECHNICAL QUALITY: Excellent, no artifacts.\n\nFINDINGS:\n\n- The coronary artery calcium score is 300, placing it in the moderate risk category according to the Agatston method. This score is in the 91st percentile for age and gender.\n- The coronary arteries are in a normal position with right coronary artery dominance.\n\nLeft Main: The left main coronary artery is a short vessel bifurcating into the LAD and LCX. In the distal segment of the left main artery, there is a mixed plaque composed of calcified and non-calcified components causing 25-30% stenosis.\n\nLAD: In the proximal segment of the LAD, there is a calcified plaque causing 25-30% stenosis. Approximately a 12 mm segment of the proximal LAD is totally occluded. The distal LAD is thought to be partially filled via collateral circulation. Diagonal branches are patent.\n\nRamus Intermedius: Observed as a developed branch, with a non-calcified plaque in the proximal segment causing 25-30% stenosis.\n\nLCX: Sequential diffuse mixed calcified and non-calcified plaques and vessel wall irregularities are observed from the proximal to mid-segment, with the most significant area showing 40-50% stenosis. In the distal LCX, a non-calcified plaque causes 60-70% stenosis. Obtuse marginal branches are patent.\n\nRCA: Approximately a 57 mm segment from the proximal to mid RCA appears occluded. The distal RCA is thought to be partially filled via collateral circulation. The posterior descending artery and posterior left ventricular branch are patent.\n\nNon-coronary cardiac findings:\n\nHeart Valves: The aortic valve is tri-leaflet. There is no thickening or calcification of the aortic and mitral valves.\n\nPericardium: The pericardial contour is preserved, with no effusion, thickening, or calcification.\n\nExtra-cardiac findings: No significant extra-cardiac findings in the limited views of the lungs, mediastinum, and upper abdomen.\n\nIMPRESSION:\n1. Stenosis: Mild (25-49%) stenosis in the left main coronary artery.\n2. Stenosis: Mild (25-49%) stenosis in the proximal segment of the LAD.\n3. Stenosis: Severe (70-99%) stenosis in the proximal segment of the LAD.\n4. Stenosis: Mild (25-49%) stenosis in the ramus intermedius.\n5. Stenosis: Moderate (50-69%) stenosis in the proximal-mid segments of the LCX.\n6. Stenosis: Severe (70-99%) stenosis in the distal segment of the LCX.\n7. Stenosis: Severe (70-99%) stenosis in the proximal-mid segments of the RCA.\n8. Plaque: Overall, a moderate amount of coronary plaque is present.\n\nRECOMMENDATION:\nCAD RADS 5: P3  \n- Consideration of invasive coronary angiography (ICA), functional and/or viability assessment.  \n- Aggressive modification of risk factors and preventive pharmacotherapy is recommended.  \n- Anti-anginal therapy and revascularization options should be evaluated according to guidelines.

CORONARY CT ANGIOGRAPHY AND CALCIUM SCORE\n\n- 74-year-old male patient with chest pain\n\nCOMPARISON: None\n\nTECHNIQUE: An initial scout scan was performed using a Canon Aquilion One scanner, followed by a coronary artery calcium protocol. Post intravenous contrast administration, images with 0.5 mm collimation were obtained along the coronary arteries. Data was transferred offline for 3D reconstructions.\n\nACQUISITION: Prospective ECG gating was used. The heart rate during imaging was approximately 62 bpm.\n\nMEDICATIONS: 100 mg of oral metoprolol was administered prior to the scan. 0.4 mg of sublingual nitroglycerin was given immediately before the scan.\n\nTECHNICAL QUALITY: Good, with minor artifacts present but diagnostic quality is maintained.\n\nFINDINGS:\n\nThe coronary artery calcium score was measured at 543, considered high risk according to the Agatston score. It is at the 89th percentile according to MESA for age and ethnicity.\n\nThe coronary arteries originate in a normal position with right coronary artery dominance.\n\nLeft Main: The left main coronary artery is a short vessel bifurcating into the LAD and LCX. No evidence of plaque or stenosis.\n\nLAD: In the proximal LAD, starting from the ostial level, an eccentrically located calcified plaque causing 40% luminal narrowing is observed. Additionally, an 18 mm Type 2 myocardial bridge is noted in the proximal LAD. In the D1 mid-segment, a millimetric calcified plaque causing less than 25% luminal narrowing is observed.\n\nLCX: In the proximal LCX, 2-3 millimetric calcified plaques causing less than 25% luminal narrowing are observed. The obtuse marginal branches are patent.\n\nRCA: In the proximal and mid RCA, millimetric calcified plaques causing 25-30% luminal narrowing are observed. In the distal segment of the RCA, a mixed (calcified and non-calcified) plaque causing up to 70% luminal narrowing is observed.\n\nNon-coronary cardiac findings:\n\nHeart Valves: The aortic valve is tri-leaflet. There is no thickening or calcification of the aortic and mitral valves.\n\nPericardium: The pericardial contour is preserved, with no effusion, thickening, or calcification.\n\nExtra-cardiac findings: No significant extra-cardiac findings in the limited views of the lungs, mediastinum, and upper abdomen.\n\nIMPRESSION:\n\n1. Stenosis: Mild (25-49%) stenosis in the proximal LAD.\n2. Myocardial Bridge: An 18 mm Type 2 myocardial bridge in the proximal LAD.\n3. Stenosis: Mild (25-49%) stenosis in the proximal LCX.\n4. Stenosis: Mild (25-49%) stenosis in the proximal and mid RCA.\n5. Stenosis: Severe (70-99%) stenosis in the distal RCA.\n6. Plaque: Overall, the coronary artery calcium score is 543, considered high risk according to the Agatston score. It is at the 89th percentile according to MESA for age and ethnicity.\n\nRECOMMENDATION: CAD RADS 4: P3/E  \n- Consider ICA (invasive coronary angiography) or functional assessment.\n- Aggressive risk factor modification and preventive pharmacotherapy are recommended.\n- Anti-anginal therapy and revascularization options should be evaluated according to guidelines.

Post intravenous contrast administration, images with 0.5 mm collimation were obtained along the coronary arteries. Data were transferred offline for 3D reconstructions.\n\nACQUISITION: Prospective ECG gating was used. The heart rate during imaging was approximately 67 bpm.\n\nMEDICATIONS: 100 mg of oral metoprolol was administered prior to the scan. 0.4 mg of sublingual nitroglycerin was given immediately before the scan.\n\nTECHNICAL QUALITY: Excellent, no artifacts.\n\nFINDINGS:\n\nThe total calcium score is zero, indicating no calcified plaque in the coronary artery tree.\n\nThe coronary arteries originate in a normal position with a co-dominant coronary artery system.\n\nLeft Main: The left main coronary artery is a short vessel bifurcating into the LAD and LCX. There is no evidence of plaque or stenosis.\n\nLAD: There is no evidence of plaque or stenosis in the left anterior descending artery. The diagonal branches are patent.\n\nLCX: There is no evidence of plaque or stenosis in the left circumflex artery. The obtuse marginal branches are patent.\n\nRCA: There is no evidence of plaque or stenosis in the right coronary artery. The posterior descending artery and posterior left ventricular branch are patent.\n\nNon-coronary cardiac findings:\n\nHeart valves: The aortic valve is trileaflet. There is no thickening or calcification of the aortic and mitral valves.\n\nPericardium: The pericardial contour is preserved, with no effusion, thickening, or calcification.\n\nExtracardiac findings: There are no significant extracardiac findings in the limited views of the lungs, mediastinum, and upper abdomen.\n\nIMPRESSION:\n\n1. Stenosis: No evidence of coronary stenosis or plaque on Coronary CT Angiography.\n2. Plaque: Overall, the total calcium score of zero indicates the absence of calcified coronary plaque.\n3. Modifiers: None.\n\nRECOMMENDATION: CAD RADS 0  \n- Assurance. Consider non-atherosclerotic causes for symptoms.

CORONARY CT ANGIOGRAPHY AND CALCIUM SCORE\n\n47-year-old patient with chest pain\nCOMPARISON: None\n\nTECHNIQUE: An initial monitoring study was performed using a Canon Aquilion One scanner, followed by the coronary artery calcium protocol. Post intravenous contrast administration, images with 0.5 mm collimation were obtained along the coronary arteries. Data was transferred offline for 3D reconstructions.\n\nACQUISITION: Prospective ECG gating was used. The heart rate during imaging was approximately 125 bpm.\n\nMEDICATIONS: 100 mg of oral metoprolol was administered prior to the scan. 0.4 mg of sublingual nitroglycerin was given immediately before the scan.\n\nTECHNICAL QUALITY: Good, with minor artifacts present but sufficient for diagnostic purposes.\n\nFINDINGS:\n\nThe total calcium score is zero, indicating no calcified plaque in the coronary vasculature.\n\nThe coronary arteries originate in a normal position. A co-dominant coronary artery system is present.\n\nLeft Main: The left main coronary artery is a short vessel bifurcating into the LAD and LCX. There is no evidence of plaque or stenosis.\n\nLAD: There is no evidence of plaque or stenosis in the left anterior descending artery. A type 1 myocardial bridge is observed in an approximately 8 mm segment of the mid LAD.\n\nLCX: Less than 25% contour irregularity is observed in the proximal LCX due to non-calcified plaque and wall irregularities.\n\nRCA: There is no evidence of plaque or stenosis in the right coronary artery. This vessel gives rise to a patent posterior descending artery and posterior left ventricular branch.\n\nNon-coronary cardiac findings:\n\nHeart Valves: The aortic valve is tri-leaflet. There is no thickening or calcification of the aortic and mitral valves.\n\nPericardium: The pericardial contour is preserved, with no effusion, thickening, or calcification.\n\nExtra-cardiac findings: No significant extra-cardiac findings in the limited views of the lungs, mediastinum, and upper abdomen.\n\nIMPRESSION:\n\n1. Stenosis: Mild (25-49%) stenosis in the proximal LCX.\n2. Type 1 myocardial bridge: Observed in the LAD.\n3. Plaque: Overall, the total calcium score is zero with no calcified coronary plaque.\n4. Modifiers: None.\n\nRECOMMENDATION: CAD RADS 1 P1 - Consider non-atherosclerotic causes for symptoms. Risk factor modification and preventive pharmacotherapy should be evaluated.
CORONARY CT ANGIOGRAPHY AND CALCIUM SCORE\n\n52-year-old patient with chest pain  \nCOMPARISON: None\n\nTECHNIQUE: An initial monitoring study was performed using a Canon Aquilion One scanner, followed by the coronary artery calcium protocol. Post intravenous contrast administration, images with 0.5 mm collimation were obtained along the coronary arteries. Data were transferred offline for 3D reconstructions.\n\nACQUISITION: Prospective ECG triggering was used. The heart rate during imaging was approximately 115 bpm.\n\nMEDICATIONS: 100 mg of oral metoprolol was administered prior to the scan. 0.4 mg of sublingual nitroglycerin was given immediately before the scan.  \nTECHNICAL QUALITY: Acceptable, with moderate artifacts present.\n\nFINDINGS:\n\nThe calcium score in the coronary arteries was measured at 42.\n\nThe coronary arteries originate in a normal position with right coronary artery dominance.\n\nLeft Main: The left main coronary artery is a short vessel bifurcating into the LAD and LCX. There is no evidence of plaque or stenosis.\n\nLAD: In the proximal LAD, there is a partially calcified plaque causing approximately 50-55% stenosis with focal calcification. There is also plaque in the D1 branch.\n\nLCX: The LCX has irregular contours with a calcified plaque in the proximal segment causing approximately 25-30% stenosis.\n\nRCA: There is no evidence of plaque or stenosis in the right coronary artery. It gives rise to the posterior descending artery and the posterior left ventricular branch.\n\nNon-coronary cardiac findings:\n\nHeart Valves: The aortic valve is trileaflet. There is no thickening or calcification of the aortic and mitral valves.\n\nPericardium: The pericardial contour is preserved, with no effusion, thickening, or calcification.\n\nExtracardiac findings: There are no significant extracardiac findings in the limited views of the lungs, mediastinum, and upper abdomen.\n\nIMPRESSION:\n\n1. Stenosis: Moderate (50-69%) stenosis in the proximal LAD.\n2. Stenosis: Mild (25-49%) stenosis in the proximal LCX.\n3. Plaque: Overall, the amount of coronary plaque is mild.\n4. Modifiers: None.\n\nRECOMMENDATION: CAD RADS 3: P1  \n- Evaluation with CT-FFR, CTP, or stress testing should be considered.  \n- Aggressive risk factor modification and preventive pharmacotherapy should be implemented.  \n- Other treatments, including guideline-directed anti-anginal therapy, should be considered.  \n- If frequent symptoms persist despite guideline-directed medical therapy, ICA (invasive coronary angiography) should be considered.

CRANIAL CT SCAN\n\nREASON FOR EXAM: Sudden onset of complete right-sided hemiparesis 4 hours ago in a patient with untreated hypertension.\n\nTECHNIQUE: Helical acquisition with fine 2mm slices without contrast injection, followed by multiplanar reconstruction study.\n\nFINDINGS:  \nSupratentorial Level:\n- A large hyperdense area measuring approximately 31.7 x 24.1 mm is observed in the left capsulo-lenticular region, with a density of 62 HU.\n- Hyperdensity extends into the frontal horns of the lateral ventricles, as well as the third and fourth ventricles.\n- There is a midline shift to the right due to mass effect.\n- Perilesional hypodensity is noted.\n- Cortical and subcortical sulci are not widened, with sulci on the left side obliterated.\n- Normal aeration of the facial sinuses.\n- No bone abnormalities detected.\n\nInfratentorial Level:\n- No density abnormalities in the brainstem or cerebellar hemispheres.\n\nCONCLUSION:\n- LEFT CAPSULO-LENTICULAR HEMATOMA WITH QUADRIVENTRICULAR EXTENSION.\n- PERILESIONAL EDEMA.\n- SUBFALCINE HERNIATION.

No abnormalities in the peripancreatic fat.\nSpleen: Normal position; enlarged, with a craniocaudal length of 107 mm.\nAdrenals: No abnormalities in position, size, or morphology.\nKidneys: No abnormalities in position, morphology, or size.\nNo deep lymphadenopathy.\nNo mesenteric fat stranding.\nNo dilation of the bowel loops.\nNo intraperitoneal fluid collection.\nBladder adequately filled with clear fluid.\n\nCONCLUSION:\n\nABDOMINAL CT SCAN SHOWS NO ORGANIC ABNORMALITIES.

PROSTATE MRI REPORT\n\nINDICATION:\nProstatic hypertrophy and elevated PSA levels.\n\nTECHNIQUE:\nSignal-weighted acquisitions including T2 in three planes, diffusion, T2 Haste in axial slices, and T1 DIXON without and with dynamic contrast enhancement using DOTAREM. \nPerformed on a SIEMENS, SIEMPRA 1.5T MRI scanner.\n\nRESULTS:\nThe prostate measures 72 x 76 x 72mm, with a volume of 196ml. \nThere is an area in the central zone showing T2 hypointensity, lateralized to the left, with diffusion hyperintensity, ADC drop, and progressive enhancement in zones 5a, 9a, and 11a (PIRADS IV). There is capsular breach and early infiltration of the surrounding fat.\nBilateral iliac and obturator lymphadenopathy measuring between 13mm and 14mm.\nNo abnormalities in the kidneys and liver.\nNo suspicious bone abnormalities.\n\nCONCLUSION:\nSignal abnormalities highly suggestive of prostatic neoplasia classified as PIRADS 4.\nSignificant prostatic hypertrophy.

ORBITAL MRI REPORT\n\nINDICATION:\nRight-sided proptosis.\n\nTECHNIQUE:\nT2-weighted, FLAIR, T2*, and T1-weighted sequences acquired in sagittal slices. Time-of-Flight sequence on the circle of Willis, sequences focused on the orbits including T1 before and after Gadolinium contrast injection.\n\nRESULTS:\nRight conical filling due to hypertrophy of all extraocular muscles and orbital fat, showing T1 and FLAIR hyperintensity with a hemorrhagic-appearing spot. No hyperintensity on diffusion sequence. This muscle-fat hypertrophy displaces the eyeball forward, causing stage III proptosis and a herniation of fatty tissue. The optic nerve is of normal size. The overall enhancement is moderate post-contrast. No abnormalities of the brain or cerebellum on the sequences performed.\n\nCONCLUSION:\nAppearance suggestive of an orbital pseudotumor; consider ruling out orbital myositis, lymphoma, or idiopathic inflammatory syndrome. Correlation with orbital muscle biopsy is recommended.

Imaging of the ankle and the lower third of the leg.\n\nRESULTS:\nThickening of the deltoid and talonavicular ligaments with high signal intensity on DP Fat Sat.\nLateral bursitis.\nMild subchondral high signal of the tibial plateau.\nEarly calcaneal spur.\nSmall talocalcaneal joint effusion.\nNo abnormalities in the subcutaneous soft tissues and muscles.\n\nCONCLUSION:\nTalo-tibial and talo-calcaneal sprain.
MRI OF THE RIGHT FOREARM\n\nINDICATION:\nEvaluation.\n\nTECHNIQUE:\nT2-weighted sequences in three planes. Difficulty positioning the patient. 1.5T SIEMENS SIEMPRA MRI.\n\nRESULTS:\nNo signal abnormalities in the bony and muscular structures of the forearm. T2 hyperintensity in the subcutaneous tissue on the dorsal side of the wrist, particularly around the periphery of the carpometacarpal joint of the first ray. Presence of joint effusion in the radiocarpal and carpometacarpal joints. Fluid infiltration around the tendons of the extensor pollicis brevis and abductor pollicis longus.\n\nCONCLUSION:\nFindings suggestive of De Quervain's tendinitis and radiocarpal and carpometacarpal arthritis. No abnormalities in the forearm.

CT SCAN OF THE CHEST\n\nINDICATION:\nEvaluation of interstitial lung disease (ILD) discovered on a chest CT angiogram performed due to suspected pulmonary embolism (PE) in the context of progressively worsening shortness of breath. No occupational exposure.\n\nPROTOCOL:\nHelical acquisition without contrast injection. These abnormalities persist in the prone position. There is also a lesser degree of ground-glass opacity in the areas affected by the reticulations. No honeycombing observed.\n\nTraction bronchiectasis in the lung bases, particularly in segment S10 on both sides. Irregular appearance of the fissures indicating signs of architectural distortion.\n\nA round and regular 8mm nodule in segment S9 of the left lung (SPF 1448).\n\nNo masses or consolidations. Mild air trapping on expiration. Moderate diffuse thickening of the bronchial walls.\n\nNo pleural or pericardial effusion.\n\nA left hilar lymph node measuring just under a centimeter, remaining oblong in shape.\n\nCONCLUSION:\nPulmonary parenchymal infiltration consistent with a probable pattern of usual interstitial pneumonia (UIP) with mild fibrosis. An 8mm nodule in segment S9 of the left lung associated with a left hilar lymph node just under a centimeter should be further evaluated with a specialist opinion and at least a follow-up CT scan in 6 months.

ABDOMINO-PELVIC SCAN\n\nREASON FOR EXAMINATION:\nThe patient has cirrhosis and is undergoing reevaluation of hypervascular liver nodules.\n\nPROCEDURE:\nA helical scan was performed on the abdomen without and with contrast injection during the arterial, portal, and delayed phases.\n\nRESULTS:\nComparison with the MRI from October 13, 2024.\n\nThe liver shows cirrhotic changes with irregular contours. There is a hypervascular nodule in the anterior part of segment IVa, currently measuring 12x12 mm, compared to 8x8 mm previously, with no washout observed, indicating a slight increase in size. No new suspicious nodules have appeared. There is no ascites. The adrenal glands, pancreas, and kidneys appear normal. There is no retroperitoneal, celiac-mesenteric, or pelvic lymphadenopathy. No abdominal or pelvic fluid collections are present.\n\nNo suspicious bone lesions were observed in the scanned area.\n\nCONCLUSION:\nCirrhotic liver. The hypervascular nodule in segment IVa has increased to 12 mm, with no washout, for which an MRI can be considered.
LIVER MRI\n\nREASON FOR EXAM:\nEvaluation of a hypervascular nodule larger than one centimeter in segment IVa, without wash-out, in a cirrhotic liver.\n\nPROCEDURE:\nMetabolic, morphological, diffusion sequences, and post-contrast imaging were performed.\n\nRESULTS:\nComparison with the CT scan from January 4, 2025.\n\nThere is a 13x13mm hepatocellular nodule located just beneath the capsule in segment IVa, which is hypervascular with slight wash-out. The nodule in segment VIII is less visible on the MRI. The liver is known to be cirrhotic.\n\nCONCLUSION:\nThe presence of wash-out in the MRI of the hypervascular hepatocellular nodule in segment IVa indicates hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC).

**Brain MRI Report**\n\n**Reason for Examination:**\nThere is a suspicion of motor neuron disease within the ALS spectrum, which has been progressing for 2 years. There are a few small, non-specific FLAIR hyperintensities in the periventricular white matter and semi-oval centers.\n\n**Conclusion:**\nThe "motor band sign" supports the presence of motor neuron disease. The goal is to rule out intracranial hypotension, which could mimic frontotemporal lobar degeneration.\n\n**Protocol:**\nThe procedure included axial diffusion sequences, SWI, 3D FLAIR, and 3D T1 gradient echo. After administering gadolinium, a 3D T1 gradient echo venous MRA was performed. Anxiolytic premedication was given in preparation for the exam.\n\n**Results:**\nThis exam was compared to the MRI from January 2021.\n\n- No recent hemorrhagic or ischemic lesions were found.\n- There is a single chronic microbleed in the right occipital region.\n- FLAIR hyperintensities in the deep and periventricular white matter suggest leukoaraiosis, likely of vascular origin (Fazekas score 1+1).\n- The bilateral frontal hygromas remain stable, with an estimated thickness of about 6 mm on the right and 8 mm on the left.\n- There is a mass effect on the adjacent brain tissue, making it difficult to identify any atrophy patterns.\n- No significant hippocampal atrophy is observed (Sheltens scale score of 1 on both sides).\n- The basal cisterns are clear, with no signs of herniation.\n- The mamillo-pontine distance remains unchanged.\n- The pituitary gland appears normal in shape.\n- There is no abnormal contrast enhancement, particularly in the meninges.\n- The dural venous sinuses and deep cerebral veins are normally patent.\n- No venous engorgement is present.\n\n**Conclusion:**\nThere is no evidence to support intracranial hypotension. The bilateral frontal hygromas remain generally stable.
**Brain MRI Report**\n\n**Indication:**\nThe patient is a 72-year-old woman being monitored for a parkinsonian syndrome that has been progressing for two years, consistent with early-stage Parkinson's disease. The goal is to rule out any signs indicative of a Multiple System Atrophy (MSA) type parkinsonian syndrome or mesencephalic atrophy.\n\n**Protocol:**\nThe MRI was conducted using 3D T1 gradient echo sequences, axial diffusion, SWI, 3D FLAIR, and neuromelanin-weighted sequences.\n\n**Results:**\n- There is no previous exam for comparison.\n- No signal or morphological abnormalities were found in the basal ganglia, midbrain, pons, or cerebellum.\n- There is a bilateral loss of visualization of nigrosome 1 on SWI. There is a moderate decrease in the spontaneous hypersignal of the FLAIR substance on the neuromelanin-weighted sequence.\n- No recent ischemic or hemorrhagic lesions were detected.\n- FLAIR hypersignals in the deep and periventricular white matter are associated with leukoaraiosis, likely of vascular origin (Fazekas score 2 + 2).\n- There is an enlargement of the ventricular system and pericerebral fluid spaces, indicating diffuse cortical and subcortical atrophy. Notably, the central sulci appear widened.\n- The appearance of an empty sella turcica is observed.\n\n**Conclusion:**\nThere is no evidence supporting a parkinsonian syndrome of the MSA or PSP type. The findings are consistent with vascular leukoaraiosis, Fazekas grade 2.
**Brain MRI Report**\n\n**Reason for Examination:**\nThis MRI was conducted to assess neurodegenerative changes and cerebral vascular load in a patient with a history of a serious road traffic accident in 2010, which resulted in a severe head injury and prolonged coma. The patient has been experiencing cognitive issues, particularly with attention, along with changes in psychological and behavioral patterns.\n\n**Procedure:**\nThe MRI included 3D T1 gradient echo sequences, axial diffusion, SWI, and 3D FLAIR sequences.\n\n**Findings:**\nThe current MRI was compared to previous scans, particularly the one from August 2012. There are no new ischemic or hemorrhagic lesions. Chronic microbleeds are mainly found above the tentorium, especially in the bilateral temporal pole regions, likely related to the past prolonged coma due to the head injury. There are FLAIR hyperintensities in the deep and periventricular white matter, suggesting a vascular origin of leukoencephalopathy (Fazekas score 2 + 1). There are post-traumatic changes in both temporal poles. The ventricular system and pericerebral fluid spaces are enlarged, particularly in the anterior region, indicating cortical and subcortical atrophy, predominantly in the frontal lobes, which was already present in 2012. There is moderate and symmetrical hippocampal atrophy (Sheltens scale 2). There is an increase in midbrain atrophy. There is focal thinning of the corpus callosum, likely related to the head injury.\n\n**Conclusion:**\nThere are no new ischemic or hemorrhagic lesions. The cortical and subcortical atrophy, predominantly in the frontal lobes, remains generally stable. There is moderate vascular leukoencephalopathy. There is an increase in midbrain subcortical atrophy.
**Brain MRI Report**\n\n**Indication:**\nA 78-year-old female patient has been experiencing walking difficulties for over a year, along with the development of weakness on the right side of her body and right-sided extrapyramidal syndrome. The examination aims to investigate the possibility of a degenerative process.\n\n**Protocol:**\nBrain level: 3D T1 gradient echo sequences, 3D FLAIR, diffusion, SWI.\n\n**Results:**\n- **Brain Level:** There is significant focal atrophy on the left side around the central region, accompanied by iron deposits in the primary motor cortex and the paracentral lobule, suggesting a degenerative process. There is also a FLAIR hyperintensity in the subcortical area of the paracentral lobule. There is bilateral and symmetrical atrophy of the superior parietal region and the precuneus. Moderate atrophy of the midbrain and superior cerebellar atrophy are noted. Additionally, there is an enlargement of the subarachnoid spaces on the brain's surface, consistent with bilateral subdural hygromas.\n- There is a symmetrical and bilateral mineral overload in the substantia nigra, associated with a loss of visibility of the nigrosomes. There is a decrease in the hyperintensity of the substantia nigra on neuromelanin-weighted sequences.\n- A single cortical microbleed is present in the left occipital region.\n- There is focal dilation of the Virchow-Robin spaces, more pronounced on the right parietal side.\n- A few small, punctate, and nodular FLAIR hyperintensities are present in the semi-oval centers, suggesting a likely vascular leukoencephalopathy, classified as FAZEKAS 1, which is stable.\n\n**Conclusion:**\nThere is marked focal atrophy in the left pericentral and paracentral regions, with cortical iron deposits and subcortical FLAIR hyperintensity. This appearance is primarily compatible with corticobasal degeneration. An associated motor neuron involvement could also be considered.

**MRI of the Pituitary Gland**\n\n**Reason for Exam:** Follow-up on a non-secreting pituitary lesion that was incidentally discovered.\n\n**Protocol:** 3D T1 spin echo sequences, coronal T2 spin echo, dynamic coronal T1 post-contrast, 3D T1 spin echo of the brain post-contrast.\n\n**Results Compared to MRI from June 12, 2022:**\n\nA nodular formation on the right side of the sella is observed, measuring 12 x 9 x 7 mm, compared to the previous 10 x 8 x 7 mm. It shows low signal intensity on T1 and T2, except for a few small fluid components seen within a defect in the pituitary gland after contrast injection. **Brain MRI Report**\n\n**Reason for Examination:**\nEvaluation of an atypical Parkinsonian syndrome that has not responded to L-dopa treatment over the past three years, accompanied by an unexplained cognitive decline.\n\n**Protocols Used:**\n3D T1 gradient echo, axial diffusion, SWI, 3D FLAIR, and neuromelanin-weighted sequences.\n\n**Findings:**\nCompared to the MRI from February 18, 2020:\n\n- There is moderate, diffuse atrophy of the cortex and subcortical areas above the tentorium and in the cerebellum, with a greater impact on the cortical regions. There is slight asymmetry, more pronounced on the left side. The cortical atrophy affects the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex, premotor and motor cortex, and the superior parietal cortex.\n- The hippocampal size is rated as Scheltens grade 2. There is no significant atrophy or signal abnormality in the midbrain, mammillary bodies, or putamen. The nigrosome is clearly visible on the left but not clearly seen on the right. The neuromelanin sequence shows preserved hypersignal in the substantia nigra.\n- There are a few non-specific FLAIR hypersignals in the periventricular and deep white matter. Two old microbleeds are noted: one in the left occipital region and one in the right midbrain. There is moderate enlargement of the intra- and pericerebral fluid spaces, consistent with the atrophy. No ischemic lesions are present. The midline structures are in place with no signs of herniation.\n\n**Conclusion:**\nThe predominant cortical atrophy does not support a diagnosis of Progressive Supranuclear Palsy (PSP) or Multiple System Atrophy (MSA). The primary cortical involvement suggests the possibility of Lewy body dementia or corticobasal degeneration, which should be correlated with clinical findings.

**Brain MRI Report**\n\n**Reason for Exam**  \nFollow-up on a nodule located in the left frontal horn in a 49-year-old female patient.\n\n**Procedure**  \nAxial diffusion sequences with ADC mapping, T1 spin echo, SWI, 3D FLAIR, BFFE, and sagittal T2 sequences were performed.\n\n**Findings**  \nThis exam was compared to the MRI from August 21, 2013.  \nThe formation is only visible on the axial FLAIR, T1, and sagittal T2 sequences that are sensitive to flow. It is not seen on the 3D FLAIR and BFFE sequences that are not sensitive to flow. This suggests that the finding is a flow artifact rather than a tissue formation.  \nThere is no recent ischemic lesion. There is no signal heterogeneity on the magnetic susceptibility sequence that would suggest a hemorrhagic lesion. There are multiple small FLAIR hyperintensities, some of which are merging, located in the semi-oval centers and deep white matter, as well as in patches in the periventricular white matter, classified as Fazekas 2+1. The size of the ventricular system is stable and does not appear dilated.\n\n**Conclusion**  \nThe described nodular formation corresponds to a flow artifact.
BRAIN MRI\n\nREASON FOR EXAMINATION\nThe patient has been experiencing cognitive issues for three years, along with a kinetic cerebellar syndrome. There are vascular risk factors present. The patient shows impairments in memory, executive functions, and motor skills.\n\nPROCEDURE\nThe patient has a right cochlear implant that is MRI-compatible. The examination was conducted using a 1.5 Tesla MRI machine, following the manufacturer's guidelines. The sequences used were T1, T2, FLAIR, diffusion, and SWI.\n\nRESULTS\nDue to significant susceptibility artifacts caused by the magnetic implant, especially affecting the study of the left hemisphere, the following observations were made: There are no T2/FLAIR signal abnormalities suggestive of vascular white matter disease. There are no morphological abnormalities in the ventricular system or subarachnoid spaces that would suggest normal pressure hydrocephalus. There is no atrophy pattern indicative of Alzheimer's disease, particularly with the preservation of the right internal temporal lobe and the precuneus.\n\nThe diffusion and susceptibility sequences could not be interpreted.\n\nCONCLUSION\nThe interpretation is limited due to significant artifacts from the right cochlear implant. With this limitation in mind, the MRI does not provide evidence supporting vascular damage, Alzheimer's disease, or normal pressure hydrocephalus.
